Title: A Panel Discussion on Google WE Scholarship
Speaker Name:
Kavya Choudhary
Ridhy Arora
Sravya Uppalavapati
Session Summary:
This panel discussion, hosted by Girls Leading Tech, provides a detailed roadmap for first-year female engineering students interested in the WE Scholarship program. The program is a two-year mentorship initiative supported by Google and managed by TalentSprint, designed to equip women with both technical and corporate skills (0:00 - 1:34).

Program Overview & Benefits:

Skill Development: The scholarship focuses on technical growth, leadership development, and preparing students for the corporate world through one-on-one mentorship with industry experts (1:07, 4:21).
Mentorship: Students gain access to guidance from experienced professionals, including those working at Google, which helps bridge the gap between academic learning and corporate expectations (4:24, 29:56).
Networking & Exposure: A highlight for many scholars is the opportunity to visit Google offices (such as the one in Hyderabad), tour the facilities, and experience corporate culture firsthand, which serves as a powerful motivator (29:56 - 32:30).
The Application & Selection Process:
The program is highly competitive, often seeing tens of thousands of applicants for a few hundred spots. The selection process typically includes four distinct stages (6:36 - 8:05):

Aptitude Test: Focuses on math and logical reasoning. Mock tests provided by the platform are highly recommended (7:16 - 7:35).
Technical Test: Candidates should be comfortable with at least one programming language (Python, Java, or C++). The questions are generally fundamental (11:12, 14:00, 18:39).
English Proficiency Test: Evaluates basic grammar, listening, and speaking skills (8:01, 16:27).
One-on-One Interview: A conversation to gauge the applicant's passion, interest in the program, and basic problem-solving abilities (8:05, 14:15).
Tips for Success:

Master the Fundamentals: You do not need advanced DSA knowledge. Focus on building strong basics in your chosen language and practice on platforms like HackerRank (11:41, 18:39).
Read Instructions Carefully: The panelists emphasized that failure to follow instructions is a common pitfall. Being detail-oriented is key (34:08, 36:11).
Consistency: Dedicating consistent time to practice alongside your college studies is more effective than last-minute cramming (34:38, 36:04).
Handling Rejection:
Panelists stressed that while the WE Scholarship is a fantastic opportunity, it does not define a student's entire career. There are many other pathways to success in tech, such as the Google STEP and SWE internships, which do not strictly require being a WE Scholar (23:16 - 27:00). Rejection should be viewed as practice and a stepping stone to future opportunities.

Title: Google Step Internship Mentorship Session
Session Summary:
This video features a comprehensive Q&A session with Jigisha Arora, a Google STEP intern, providing valuable insights into the internship application process, interview preparation, and the overall Google intern experience.

Overview of Google STEP Internship
Purpose: The STEP (Student Training in Engineering Program) is designed for second-year undergraduate students, offering an early opportunity to gain industry experience (03:00).
Conversion: A significant highlight is that 90-95% of STEP interns are typically converted into SWE (Software Engineering) interns for their third year, increasing the chances of landing a full-time offer (03:31).
Eligibility: While primarily for second-year B.Tech/BE students, Jigisha encourages students to apply regardless of minor eligibility concerns, as the experience is invaluable (20:10-21:25).
Application & Interview Process
Referrals: For off-campus applications, a referral from a Google employee is highly recommended to ensure the application is reviewed amidst a high volume of submissions (04:42, 11:35). Jigisha suggests reaching out to alumni or Google employees via LinkedIn (23:41).
Assessment Rounds: The process typically includes resume submission, an online assessment (OA), and two rounds of technical interviews (05:24).
Technical Preparation:
DSA focus: Interviews are heavily based on Data Structures and Algorithms (DSA) (05:53, 42:52).
Resources: Jigisha recommends Striver’s sheets for structured practice (06:44-08:04). She emphasizes covering a wide variety of topics rather than deep-diving into advanced algorithms (08:16).
Approach: During interviews, focus on providing an optimal solution rather than coding a brute-force approach (18:46). Practice writing clean, readable code and be prepared to discuss time complexity (17:28-19:37).
Internship Life at Google
Projects: Interns are assigned random projects and tech stacks, with initial time allocated for learning (38:37).
Work Environment: The culture is described as supportive and fun, with access to office amenities like gyms, nap rooms, and micro-kitchens (47:55-48:55).
Networking: The speaker encourages engaging with other tech opportunities simultaneously, such as hackathons and internships at companies like PayPal, Flipkart, and Salesforce (34:12-35:45).

Title: Google Summer of Code Mentorship Session
Session Summary:
This video is a comprehensive guide to Google Summer of Code (GSOC), featuring insights from Nishant Singhal (GSOC 2022 contributor and 2023 mentor) and Angel Sharma (GSOC 2024 contributor).

1. What is GSOC and its Timeline?
Definition: GSOC is a global, online program focused on bringing new contributors into open-source software development [01:48].

Timeline for 2025:

December 10: Program announced [05:32].

February 5: Deadline for organizations to apply [05:51].

February 19: List of accepted organizations is published [06:04].

March 17 – April 1: Period for student proposal submissions [06:11].

May: Results and slot allocations are announced [06:32].

June – September: The coding period, which can last 3 or 6 months depending on the project [06:40].

2. Selecting an Organization and Project
Filtering: Use tools like gsoc-organizations.dev to filter by tech stack (e.g., React, Django, Flutter) and past participation history [07:32].

Activity Check: Visit the organization's communication channels (Slack, Discord, IRC). If the channel is "dead," the organization might not participate this year [08:48].

Starting Small: Look for "Good First Issues" in the GitHub repository. These are beginner-friendly bugs meant for newcomers [10:23].

Research First: Before asking questions, read the CONTRIBUTING.md file. Mentors expect you to do your own research before seeking help [10:47].

3. The Proposal Process
Structure: A strong proposal includes background information, project deliverables, a detailed implementation plan, and a realistic timeline [41:45].

Timeline Importance: Mentors focus heavily on the timeline. You must include buffer time for exams and personal commitments (like festivals) [47:55].

Review: It is highly recommended to get your proposal reviewed by mentors at least 2–3 times before the final submission [18:03].

Quantity: Submitting 2–3 proposals across different organizations is a safe strategy to increase your chances [35:46].

4. Benefits and Stipends
Stipends: Based on project size: Small ($750), Medium ($1,500), and Large ($3,000) [32:43].

Career Growth: GSOC is highly valued by recruiters. Some mentors even offer job opportunities or internships (e.g., at Meta or JetBrains) to high-performing contributors [12:18].

Networking: You gain access to a global community of developers and may be invited to international conferences [12:05].

5. Key Tips for Success
Consistency: Start contributing early (even in December/January) to build rapport with the community [18:24].

Communication: Don't just submit code; talk to the mentors and be active in the community [13:09].

Language Proficiency: You don't need to be an expert. If you understand basic logic, you can learn the specific syntax of a new language while contributing [23:58].

Title: Google SWE Internship Mentorship Session
Session Summary:
This video is a detailed mentorship session by Divina Pooja John, a Software Engineer at Google, focused on preparing for Google internships (SWE, STEP) and the Women Engineers (WE) program.

1. Key Internship & Scholarship Opportunities
The session outlines three primary paths for students at different stages of their engineering degree [07:15]:

Google SWE Internship: A 3-month summer internship for 3rd-year students. Hiring primarily happens in July-August, with a second round in January-February [07:23].

STEP Internship: Targeted at 2nd-year students (after the 4th semester). Selection typically occurs during the 3rd or beginning of the 4th semester [08:34].

Google WE (Women Engineers) Program: A 2-year training scholarship for 1st-year students to build a competitive technical profile [09:07].

2. The Selection Process
On-Campus vs. Off-Campus: Google visits select high-profile colleges. For others, students must apply via the Google Careers page [10:12].

Online Assessment (OA): Usually involves two coding questions to be solved in 60 minutes [10:58].

Interviews: Focus almost exclusively on Data Structures and Algorithms (DSA). They typically consist of two technical rounds, with a third round only if the interviewers are undecided [12:04].

WE Program Specifics: Includes an aptitude round (logical reasoning), a language proficiency round, a coding round, and a fitment interview [13:48].

3. Preparation Strategies
Intuition-Based Learning: Divina emphasizes moving beyond rote memorization. Instead of just "learning the code," focus on why a specific data structure is chosen and how to optimize for time and space complexity [41:10].

Language Choice: While Java and C++ are popular, Divina notes that Python is perfectly acceptable as long as you understand its internal workings (e.g., the time complexity of built-in functions like len()) [01:04:16].

DSA Sheets & Contests: Using structured sheets (like Striver’s or NeetCode) is helpful, but participating in timed contests (LeetCode weekly/bi-weekly) is crucial to simulate the pressure of an actual OA [49:33].

Projects: Aim for unique projects that solve real-world problems. Research-based projects or those with measurable impact (e.g., "improved efficiency by 30%") stand out more than generic "copy-paste" projects [31:56].

4. Resume Tips
Use a professional template (ideally LaTeX via Overleaf) and keep it to one page [56:19].

List skills in descending order of proficiency [57:05].

Include quantifiable achievements (ranks in competitions, percentages, or user metrics) [57:40].

5. Balancing Development and DSA
Divina suggests a split approach: focus on DSA during weekdays for interview readiness and dedicate weekends to development/projects to build a strong portfolio [01:06:19]. For 1st-year students, she encourages exploring various domains (Web, App, AI/ML) before narrowing down a specialization in the 2nd year [01:01:30].

Title: Uber She++ & Uber Star Internship Mentorship Session
Session Summary:
This video is a detailed mentorship session by Radhika Bansal, an incoming software engineer at Uber, who explains the entire process of landing the Uber She++ and Uber Star Internship.

Below is a detailed summary of the key sections discussed in the video:

1. Uber She++ vs. Uber Star Internship
The speaker clarifies the difference between these two often-confused programs:

Uber She++: A professional development networking event. It is a one-day conference where students can interact with Uber’s leadership, HR, and engineers to understand the company's culture and engineering challenges [02:14].

Uber Star Internship: The actual 2-month summer internship. You cannot apply for this directly; only those shortlisted for the She++ event are eligible to interview for the Star Internship [08:13].

2. Eligibility and Applications
Target Audience: Primarily 2nd-year B.Tech students (or 3rd-year students in dual-degree programs) [02:52].

Inclusivity: Open to female students, the LGBTQ+ community, persons with disabilities (PWD), and students from economically weaker sections (EWS) [02:59].

Off-Campus Opportunity: Previously limited to about 20 top colleges, Uber has now opened applications to off-campus students as well [04:10].

3. The Selection Process
Online Assessment (OA): Unlike many companies, Uber does not shortlist resumes before the test. Everyone who meets the eligibility criteria usually gets a test link [03:30].

Test Format: Typically a 90-minute test on CodeSignal. It includes 3–4 Data Structures and Algorithms (DSA) questions. Topics often include DP, Graphs, Stacks, and Hashing [04:36].

Hiring Manager (HM) Interview: If you are among the top 75–100 performers in the OA, you are invited to She++ and receive an interview call. This round focuses on your projects, development mindset, and behavioral traits rather than live coding [09:57].

4. Internship Perks and Benefits
Stipend: One of the highest for 2nd-year students, increasing annually. For the upcoming batch, the speaker mentions it is approximately ₹1.2 Lakhs per month [09:05].

Networking: The She++ event includes "Fireside Chats" with women leaders and an "Ideathon" where students brainstorm new features for the Uber app with mentors [06:29].

Logistics: Uber handles all travel, flights, and stay for the conference [05:41].

5. Resume and Interview Tips
Project Impact: Focus on building projects that solve real-world problems rather than simple "clones" of existing apps [23:48].

The STAR Method: Use the Situation, Task, Action, Result framework to explain your experiences during the interview [13:25].

Know the Company: Align your answers with Uber’s values, such as "Great minds don't think alike" [17:11].

Resume Format: Use Overleaf for a professional appearance. Ensure your GitHub and LinkedIn links are present [01:08:05].

6. Timeline Expectations
Applications: Generally open around December [33:52].

Online Assessment: Usually takes place in late January [34:14].

Event & Interviews: The She++ event happens about a month after OA results, with internship interviews following shortly after [34:35].

Title: LinkedIn Masterclass
Session Summary:
This is a comprehensive summary of the LinkedIn Masterclass hosted by Manik (@themanikdiaries) for the "Girls Leading Tech" community. The session is a deep dive into optimizing a LinkedIn profile to attract opportunities and build a professional brand.

1. Profile Fundamentals: The "First Impression" Elements
Manik emphasizes that the first four things a visitor sees are your photo, banner, name, and headline. These must be optimized to ensure the user scrolls further.

Profile Picture [21:24]: Use a clear, high-quality headshot with a smile to project positivity. Avoid passport-size photos or selfies. Solid backgrounds (like yellow) help catch attention.

Banner/Header [15:43]: Never leave it empty. Use it to reflect your personality or achievements. For example, include photos from events you've attended or posters that showcase your niche (e.g., software engineering).

Headline [24:54]: It should be concise and descriptive. Avoid excessive emojis [26:00]. Mention your current roles, certifications, or aspirations (e.g., "Open Source Contributor" or "Aspiring Software Engineer").

2. Strategic Profile Sections
About Section [38:57]: Write in clear paragraphs (first or third person). Include your strengths, current work, and personal interests (hobbies like reading or traveling) to humanize your profile.

Experience & The "Number Game" [45:05]: Use metrics to show impact. Instead of just listing duties, say "Increased efficiency by 20%" or "Managed a team of 10." Numbers provide credibility and attract recruiters.

Recommendations [32:56]: Seek one or two high-quality recommendations per year from managers or seniors rather than many from peers.

Custom URL [01:03:23]: Personalize your LinkedIn URL (e.g., linkedin.com/in/yourname) to look professional.

3. Content Strategy & Networking
What to Post [56:13]: Share your journey, hackathon wins, event experiences, or learnings from sessions like this masterclass.

The "Hook" [55:45]: The first two lines of your post are critical to stop the scroll.

Building Friends, Not Just Connections [01:15:20]: Instead of just adding people, engage with their content via "Valuable Comments" [01:14:58]. A 5-minute thoughtful comment is the best first step to building a real connection.

Personalized Notes [01:16:53]: When sending connection requests to high-value individuals, always include a note explaining why you want to connect.

4. Advanced LinkedIn Tools
SSI Score (Social Selling Index) [01:21:57]: This score reflects how well your profile is optimized to be found by recruiters. A score above 50 is generally good for increasing visibility in "Sales Navigator" searches.

Job Seeking [01:35:50]: Don't just "Easy Apply." Find the hiring manager or employees at the company and reach out with a personalized message to increase your chances.

LinkedIn Premium [43:56]: Recommended primarily for third or fourth-year students actively seeking jobs/internships due to the "InMail" features and increased reach.

5. The "North Star" Metric [39:50]
Manik advises aligning your entire profile—headline, bio, and posts—toward a single niche (e.g., Healthcare, Web3, or Cloud). Diversifying too much early on makes it harder for recruiters to categorize you.

6. Profile Reviews & Live Advice
The latter half of the video features live critiques of attendee profiles:

Sunjin [01:49:04]: Advised to use numbers in the experience section and make the banner more readable.

Arushi [01:53:07]: Suggested adding GitHub links for projects and starting a posting habit.

Shubhi [02:13:20]: Encouraged starting or joining at least one community/society in college to build leadership experience.

Key Takeaway: LinkedIn is not just a digital resume; it is an "Opportunity Magnet" [01:00:01]. By being active, using numbers to prove impact, and engaging authentically, you can land roles and mentorships without traditional applications.

Title: Linkedin Coachin Mentorship Session
Session Summary:
This video is a detailed mentorship session hosted by Girls Leading Tech, featuring Swasti Sarum (a LinkedIn CoachIn '24 mentee and upcoming JPMC intern). She provides a comprehensive guide on how to navigate the LinkedIn CoachIn program, from the application stage to securing an internship.

1. Program Overview & Eligibility
What it is: A 4–6 month technical mentorship program designed to prepare female engineering students for Software Development Engineer (SDE) roles [04:31].

Eligibility: Open to 2nd-year B.Tech and 3rd-year Integrated Dual Degree (IDD) female students [05:18].

Focus: Purely technical, focusing on Data Structures and Algorithms (DSA) and problem-solving [05:18].

Key Perk: Participants who complete the mentorship are eligible for a pre-internship interview for the following summer (e.g., 2026) [04:54].

2. The Selection Process (Online Test & Interview)
The first major hurdle is the Online Assessment (OA), typically hosted on HackerRank [07:05].

Test Structure (120 Minutes):

Technical Section: 3 coding questions (Medium level). Topics often include Graphs, Dynamic Programming (DP), Sliding Window, Stacks, and Queues [07:20].

Subjective Section: A "Statement of Purpose" (SOP) style question: "Why do you want to be a part of the CoachIn program?" [10:10].

Proctoring: The test is proctored with cameras on. Tab switching and copy-pasting are disabled [09:28].

Recruiter Screening Call: After clearing the test, candidates have a 10–15 minute call. This is non-technical and focuses on your motivation, adaptability, and interest in the program [27:41].

3. Mentorship Journey (The "CoachIn" Experience)
Once selected, the program is divided into structured learning blocks:

One-on-One Mentoring: Each mentee is paired with a LinkedIn employee for personalized career and technical guidance [38:53].

Monthly Sessions: Monthly deep-dives into specific DSA topics followed by short coding tests [38:10].

Mock Interviews: Conducted by LinkedIn engineers (other than your primary mentor) to provide unbiased feedback on your technical and communication skills [40:12].

Fireside Chats: Interactions with industry leaders and past mentees to understand corporate culture [40:40].

LinkedIn Premium: Mentees receive 6 months of LinkedIn Premium for free [41:25].

4. How to Transition to an Internship
The mentorship itself does not guarantee an internship.

In June (after the mentorship), mentees must register for the Internship Opportunity [45:21].

This involves another coding round and two interview rounds (Technical and HR/Group Discussion) [45:37].

5. Key Advice for Applicants
Prepare the SOP early: Since the subjective question is almost always the same, write it beforehand to save time for coding during the test [12:06].

Attempt the Sample Test: Familiarize yourself with the HackerRank environment to reduce anxiety on the actual test day [07:45].

Focus on Logic: Even if you can't pass all test cases, submit your partial code. Points are often awarded for progress [35:50].

Resource Recommendation: Swasti recommends using the Striver SDE Sheet (191 questions) for a solid DSA foundation in a short time [21:19].

Title: Blockchain Fundamentals
Session Summary:
This mentorship session, titled "Blockchain Fundamentals," is led by Mohammad Mudassir, a software engineer at the Algorand Foundation and former researcher at IIT Bombay. He provides a comprehensive overview of blockchain technology, its history, and its future.

1. Evolution of the Internet
Mudassir begins by tracing the history of the web to explain why blockchain is necessary:

Web 1.0 (The Read-Only Web): Characterized by static websites like early Wikipedia or digital newspapers. Users could consume content but not interact or create [04:58].

Web 2.0 (The Social Web): The current era of dynamic, interactive apps like Instagram, Twitter, and YouTube. While it allows content creation, it is centralized, meaning big corporations own and can delete user data [07:08].

Web 3.0 (The Decentralized Web): The future of the internet where users have ownership of their data through decentralized technologies, providing more privacy and censorship resistance [11:46].

2. What is Blockchain?
He defines blockchain using four key pillars: Distributed, Immutable, Transparent, and Ledger [16:38].

Distributed: Data is stored across multiple computers (nodes) rather than a single central server [17:26].

Immutable: Once data is written to the blockchain, it cannot be changed or deleted [18:41].

Transparent: All transactions are visible and traceable on a public ledger [17:47].

Ledger: A digital "record book" that tracks all activities and transactions [19:15].

3. Core Concepts and Mechanisms
Consensus Protocols: Since there is no central "manager," the network uses rules called consensus algorithms (like Proof of Work for Bitcoin and Proof of Stake for Ethereum) to ensure all nodes agree on the state of the ledger [33:52].

Smart Contracts: These are self-executing programs stored on the blockchain. Once deployed, they are immutable and automatically handle transactions based on "if-then" logic [25:40].

DAOs (Decentralized Autonomous Organizations): Organizations governed by smart contracts rather than a central leadership, where community members use tokens to vote on decisions [43:44].

4. Major Blockchains Explained
Bitcoin: Created by Satoshi Nakamoto in response to the 2008 financial crisis to allow peer-to-peer financial interaction without banks [29:32].

Ethereum: Introduced the ability to run programs (Smart Contracts) on the blockchain, moving beyond just simple payments [31:38].

Algorand: Focuses on solving the "Blockchain Trilemma" (balancing security, scalability, and decentralization) using a unique Pure Proof of Stake mechanism [39:53].

5. Career Advice and Resources
In the Q&A session, Mudassir shares tips for aspiring developers:

Tech vs. Crypto: He emphasizes focusing on Blockchain Technology as a career rather than the volatile cryptocurrency market [54:31].

Learning Resources: He recommends the YouTube channel "Code Eater" for beginners, the book "Mastering Bitcoin," and the interactive platform "CryptoZombies" for learning Solidity [45:52].

Building a Portfolio: He encourages students to participate in hackathons, contribute to Open Source projects, and share their progress on LinkedIn to gain visibility [51:19].

The session concludes with an invitation for students to explore the field further and participate in upcoming developer meetups and internships at the Algorand Foundation [01:01:22].

Title: Outreachy Mentorship Session
Session Summary:
This video is a detailed mentorship session by Siddhi Agarwal, a 2024 Outreachy intern with the OCaml organization. She provides a comprehensive guide on the Outreachy internship program, covering the application process, selection criteria, and personal strategies for success.

Overview of Outreachy & Open Source
Mission: Outreachy promotes diversity in tech by providing open-source internships to people who are underrepresented in the tech industry where they live [01:44].

Open Source Concept: Siddhi explains open source as software that is free to use and modify, using Linux as a primary example [02:15].

Inclusivity: The program isn't just for developers; projects range from UX/UI and data science to non-tech fields like marketing and event planning [04:44].

The Application Process
The process consists of two main stages:

Initial Application (Round 1): Starts in early February and involves four essay questions [03:33]. About 1,000 to 2,000 applicants are typically selected to move forward [04:10].

Contribution Period (Round 2): Selected applicants contribute to open-source projects for one month. Mentors evaluate these contributions to choose the final interns [04:25].

Final Selection: Only about 80 applicants worldwide are selected for the actual paid internship [08:46].

Key Tips for the Essay Questions
Use Data: Don't just say you are underrepresented; use numbers. Siddhi used percentages of women in her college and in high-level industry positions to support her claims [06:53].

Cite Sources: If you provide data, include links to the source [07:33].

Personal Narratives: Share specific stories of discrimination or bias you have faced to convince the organizers of your unique situation [08:05].

Early Submission: The initial applications are often processed on a first-come, first-served basis, so apply as early as possible [41:05].

Strategies for the Contribution Period
Project Selection: Choose projects that interest you. Siddhi chose OCaml despite not knowing the language because she found the community and the challenge intriguing [12:15].

Be Proactive: Start contributing within the first 1-2 days. Some projects "close for contributors" early if they get too many applicants [26:42].

Communication: Ask for help. Open-source communities are helpful, but you must show effort by checking the "ReadMe" files before asking basic questions [25:50].

Soft Skills: Mentors look for a good attitude. Helping other applicants with setup issues can make you stand out [28:21].

Eligibility and Rules
Time Commitment: You must have a two-month holiday or break period to participate. Outreachy requires a full-time commitment and does not allow you to hold another job or internship simultaneously [29:30].

Outreachy vs. GSoC: If you are selected for Google Summer of Code (GSoC), you cannot do Outreachy at the same time. Furthermore, completing a GSoC internship makes you permanently ineligible for Outreachy [38:42].

Q&A Highlights
Resume: Not strictly required but can be attached to the final project proposal [40:38].

DSA: There are no Data Structures and Algorithms (DSA) rounds in the selection process [21:06].

Skill Level: You don't need to be an expert. Many projects list "low" skill requirements, allowing you to learn as you contribute [24:38].

Title: Google Solution Challenge Mentorship Session
Session Summary:
This is a detailed summary of the mentorship session on the Google Solution Challenge, featuring Krishna Aute, a previous winner of the competition. He shares his experience, strategies, and technical advice for students looking to succeed in the 2025 challenge.

1. Competition Overview & 2025 Changes
National Focus: Krishna notes a major change this year: the competition has shifted from a direct global scale to a National level initial stage [03:47]. In India, 100 teams will be selected across regional boot camps, which will then be narrowed down to the top 10 and finally 5 winners [06:21].

SDGs and Problem Statements: The challenge is based on the 17 United Nations Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs) [04:08]. This year, Google has provided 31 specific problem statements to choose from, though students can still propose their own innovative ideas [09:02].

Timeline: Registration runs from January 6 to March 6, with project submissions due by late March [09:38].

2. Krishna’s Winning Project: "Spoon Share"
Krishna details his project, Spoon Share, which won the challenge by addressing "Zero Hunger" and "No Poverty" [17:42].

Core Functionality: The app connects surplus food (from events or individuals) with people in need via NGOs [25:54].

Key Features: * Verified Listings: NGOs verify food quality before it appears on the map [26:10].

Zero Waste: Integrated recycling plants process food that is no longer fit for consumption into bio-fertilizers [18:03].

Interactive Mapping: A map view helps users find free food locations nearby [26:23].

3. Building a Winning Team & Product
Team Composition: A team should be 2–4 members [16:45]. Krishna advises against picking just friends; instead, look for diverse skill sets, including non-tech members for documentation and user research [19:14].

Product vs. Project: He emphasizes treating the entry as a product that evolves through continuous feedback, rather than a one-time school project [18:50].

User Research (R&D): Don't just build; go to the "ground level." Krishna's team visited smart city offices and interviewed potential users to validate their ideas [30:34].

4. Technical Advice & Presentation Tips
Google Technologies: To win, you must use Google tools. He recommends Flutter for the front end, Firebase for the database/auth, and Google Cloud [22:33].

Focus on AI: This year’s challenge heavily favors AI. Krishna suggests using Gemma, Gemini, or Vertex AI [23:07].

Effective Pitching: * Numbers Matter: Use statistics (e.g., "22.5 million people suffer from hunger") to prove the problem's scale [28:45].

Visuals: Use bullet points and icons instead of long paragraphs in slides [38:11].

Architecture Diagrams: Use tools like eraser.io to create professional-grade technical architectures [40:10].

Figma Prototypes: If your app isn't fully coded, a high-fidelity Figma prototype with interactive "threading" can still be very impactful for initial rounds [54:05].

5. Prizes and Incentives
Prize Pool: The total prize pool for this year is 8 lakh rupees [14:07].

Certifications: Top 100 teams receive Google Cloud certification vouchers ($100+ value) and exclusive "swag kits" [14:27].

Mentorship: Shortlisted teams get direct access to Google mentors from across the globe (e.g., Canada, Nigeria, India) to refine their solutions [11:27].

Title: Google Girl Hackathon Mentorship Session
Session Summary:
This video is an extensive webinar featuring Tanisha Singh, the winner of the Google Girl Hackathon (GGH) 2024. She shares her comprehensive journey, technical strategies, and practical advice for female engineering students aiming to excel in high-stakes hackathons.

The Google Girl Hackathon Structure
Tanisha breaks down the competition into three primary phases [04:44]:

Resume Shortlisting: The initial screening. She emphasizes using a professional, one-page format that highlights academic background, technical skills, and projects [05:41].

Google Online Challenge (GOC): A critical elimination round consisting of two coding questions to be solved in 60 minutes. Tanisha describes the difficulty level as LeetCode Medium-Hard to Hard [08:39].

The Hackathon Phase: A solo competition where participants choose from specific problem statements (e.g., Healthcare, Education, Disaster Relief) to build a functional prototype [10:33].

Winning Project & Technical Execution
Tanisha chose the Disaster Relief domain and developed a multi-feature AI solution [14:47]:

Early Wildfire Detection using satellite imagery.

Flood Victim Detection and water level classification from drone images.

Damage Assessment of floods using satellite data.

Key Technical Insights:

Research is Paramount: She spent 2 of the 4 allotted days purely on research to ensure her solution addressed real-world gaps [13:18].

Model Training: She fine-tuned YOLOv8 models and used Roboflow for custom datasets [28:57].

Deployment: Due to time constraints, she used Streamlit for the frontend and hosted models on Google Cloud and Roboflow [34:01].

Accuracy Metrics: She stressed the importance of using appropriate metrics like mAP (Mean Average Precision) for object detection and AUC (Area Under Curve) for segmentation [33:34].

Final Presentation & Judging
The finale involves a 7-minute presentation followed by a 3-minute Q&A [20:23].

Edge Cases: Juries often ask about scalability and design flaws. Tanisha shares an example where she had to justify how drones would fly in poor visibility during floods, leading her to research IP45-rated drones [24:44].

Communication: She advises speaking clearly and avoiding overly "fancy" words. The goal is to demonstrate the impact and novelty of the solution rather than just technical complexity [21:43].

Advice for Different Academic Years
For 1st & 2nd Years: Don't stress over a "perfect" resume. Focus on participating to gain experience. Even if you only know HTML/CSS, build something to understand the "culture" of tech competitions [52:36].

For 3rd & 4th Years: Focus on advanced DSA (Graphs, DP, Heaps) for the coding round and aim for projects with high innovation or cost-reduction potential [43:54].

Recommended Learning Resources
DSA: Consistent practice on LeetCode, specifically participating in timed contests to simulate the pressure of the Online Challenge [09:49].

AI/ML: Tanisha recommends Krish Naik’s YouTube playlists for basics and emphasizes learning by reading IEEE research papers to find unsolved problems [01:02:28].

Title: UI/UX Day
Resume Day
Session Summary:
This detailed summary covers a comprehensive workshop by Radhika Patwari (SDE at Uber, Ex-Google) on crafting high-impact resumes, specifically tailored for students and early-career professionals.

1. Choosing the Right Platform [02:34]
Overleaf (Recommended): Radhika strongly suggests using Overleaf (LaTeX) over Canva or Resume.io. It offers the most professional look, flexibility, and is highly favored by recruiters in tech [04:53].

Google Docs: Useful for drafting and backups, but the final version should ideally be in LaTeX for a polished finish [52:10].

2. Essential Resume Sections [13:06]
Headline: Your name should be in a larger, bold font. Include a professional email, phone number (with country code), and hyperlinks to your LinkedIn and GitHub profiles [15:19].

Education: Place this at the top for students. Include your degree, full college name (avoid acronyms), GPA (specify the scale, e.g., 8.5/10), and your expected graduation date [18:48].

Work Experience: Includes internships, freelancing, and open-source contributions. Use the STAR method to describe your impact [20:54].

Skills: Categorize skills into "Languages," "Frameworks/Libraries," and "Tools" (e.g., Git, Docker) [37:34].

3. The STAR Method & Impact Quantification [56:05]
Radhika emphasizes that descriptions must focus on results, not just tasks:

Situation: The context of the problem.

Task: What you were challenged to do.

Action: The specific steps or technologies you used.

Result: The outcome, quantified with numbers [56:24].

Example: Instead of saying "Optimized code," say "Reduced project delivery delays by 20% by implementing CI/CD pipelines" [01:00:01].

4. Optimizing for ATS (Applicant Tracking Systems) [01:03:46]
90% of large companies use software to filter resumes. To pass:

Keywords: Use terms found in the job description (e.g., "Python," "Distributed Systems") [12:43].

Simple Formatting: Avoid complex graphics, photos, or multi-column layouts that confuse the software [48:42].

Standard Fonts: Use clean fonts like Arial or Calibri. Avoid fancy, decorative fonts [51:49].

File Format: Always save and upload as a PDF [54:11].

5. Professional "Pro-Tips" for Success [01:04:33]
One-Page Limit: Keep the resume to a single page unless you have 5+ years of experience [42:08].

Active Links: Double-check that your email, LinkedIn, and GitHub links actually work [01:04:49].

No Redundancy: Avoid starting every bullet point with the same verb (e.g., "Worked on..."). Use varied action verbs like "Designed," "Spearheaded," or "Automated" [34:38].

Reverse Chronological Order: Always list your most recent experience first [01:08:19].

Thorough Knowledge: Never list a project or skill you cannot explain in depth during an interview. You must be able to discuss every word on your resume [01:10:01].

6. Handling a "Thin" Resume [01:01:21]
For first or second-year students without internships:

Focus on Coursework (list relevant subjects like Data Structures) [41:21].

Include Hackathons (even if you didn't win, describe what you built) [24:43].

Highlight Leadership Roles in college societies or volunteer work [42:27].

Conclusion
The core message is that a resume is your "identity" in the hiring process [11:40]. It must be well-structured, easy for a machine to read, and demonstrate your passion through quantifiable achievements rather than just a list of responsibilities.

Title: Hackathon Day
Session Summary:
This video features Mohit Bhat, a 50x hackathon winner and industry expert, sharing a comprehensive guide on how to successfully navigate and win hackathons. He breaks down the journey from a beginner's perspective to advanced strategies for international competitions.

What are Hackathons?
Mohit defines hackathons not as cybersecurity "hacking" events, but as intensive building events [03:53].

The Goal: Form a team, brainstorm a unique idea, and build a functional prototype (MVP) within a limited timeframe (usually 24–48 hours) [04:58].

Accessibility: They are open to all backgrounds, including developers, designers, and product managers [06:04].

Core Benefits of Participating
Rapid Learning: You can learn a new tech stack (like Blockchain or AI) in 24 hours because of the high-pressure environment [09:59].

Career Growth: Hackathon projects are superior to generic "library management" semester projects for resumes [21:14]. Mohit notes that many companies (like Flipkart or Goldman Sachs) hire directly from these events [20:11].

Networking & Rewards: Beyond prize money (which can range from thousands to crores in Web3), you gain access to mentors, industry leaders, and even potential startup investments [15:45], [24:33].

Strategies for Winning
Mohit emphasizes that winning is a blend of technical execution and "hacking" the presentation:

The "Unbeatable" Team: Ideally consists of a dedicated frontend developer, a backend developer, and a strong presenter (who should also be an engineer) [38:39].

The Unique Selling Point (USP): Judges look for something unique. For example, don’t just build a chat app; build a "decentralized, temper-proof chat app" using blockchain [41:20].

Visual Impact: A working frontend and a polished demo often matter more than a complex backend that can't be seen [44:36].

The Pitch: Use an Architecture Diagram. A clear flow diagram (using tools like draw.io) helps judges understand the technical depth of your project quickly [01:03:09], [01:10:54].

Tech Stack Recommendations
For those starting out, Mohit suggests focusing on:

Backend: Node.js (highly preferred due to extensive library support in hackathons) [46:57].

Frontend: React or Next.js [48:31].

App Dev: Flutter or React Native (building a mobile app often gives you a competitive edge over web apps) [49:55].

Modern Tools: Leverage AI like ChatGPT or Claude to speed up frontend coding and brainstorming [01:15:37].

Finding Opportunities
He lists several key platforms to track:

Devfolio: Best for Indian college-level hackathons [33:36].

Devpost & ETHGlobal: For international and high-stakes online events [33:41], [34:55].

Unstop & HackerEarth: Common for corporate hiring hackathons [34:30].

Twitter: Essential for finding "Hacker Houses" and international travel grants [58:55].

Smart India Hackathon (SIH) Tips
As an SIH judge, Mohit advises [01:01:31]:

Selection: Choose "Hard" problem statements to face less competition [01:05:04].

Submission: Always include a GitHub link and a Video Demo in your initial PPT, even if not explicitly asked [01:04:34].

Grand Finale: Be prepared to code on the fly. Judges will ask for specific feature changes during the event to test if you actually built the project [01:04:12].

Title: LinkedIn Day
Networking Day
Session Summary:
The video, titled "How you can build a strong Network | By Garima Pahwa," is a detailed session hosted by the "Girls Leading Tech" community. The guest speaker, Garima Pahwa, a 4th-year college student and employee at Becton Dickinson, shares her practical journey and strategies for leveraging networking to land job opportunities and build a career in tech.

1. Defining Networking
More than just "Connections": Garima defines networking not just as talking to people or making "real" connections, but as building professional friendships that provide mutual benefit [01:10].

The Goal-Oriented Approach: Real networking occurs when you get something tangible out of an interaction, such as finding a project partner, a teammate for a hackathon, or a job referral [09:14].

2. Strategic Networking for Students
Start with College Clubs: In the first and second years, students should join college societies to learn collaboration and boost confidence [05:03].

The "Rule of Five": Garima recommends a personal rule: whenever attending an event, exchange LinkedIn IDs with at least five people and ensure those names stay in your head for future collaboration [06:58].

Hackathons as Networking Hubs: She emphasizes that hackathons are the best places for networking because you are actively building projects with others [07:31].

Balancing Academics and Community: Garima follows a strict priority list: 1) Internships/Jobs, 2) GPA, and 3) Community involvement. She took a complete break from community events during her placement season to focus on technical skills [41:09].

3. Leveraging Online Platforms
LinkedIn vs. X (Twitter): * LinkedIn is essential for being visible to HRs and recruiters [13:06].

X (Twitter) is highly effective for the Web3 space and for directly reaching out to startup founders by "building in public" [11:46].

Building in Public: Garima shares how she landed a job offer from Push Protocol simply by posting her progress and projects on social media, which caught the founder's attention [11:38].

Online Success Stories: She cites an example of a student (Satyam) who won a NASA-hosted global hackathon purely through online participation and networking, proving that physical location isn't a barrier to success [34:11].

4. Maintaining and Retaining Connections
Value Exchange: To keep a connection alive after an event, talk about specific projects or technical stacks (e.g., ML libraries, frameworks). Exchanging resources, like links to courses (e.g., Alchemy University for Web3), makes the connection memorable and beneficial [25:07].

The "Click" Moment: Aim to find a specific topic of interest that "clicks" during a conversation so that when you see a relevant opportunity later, you immediately remember that person [24:30].

5. Overcoming Social Barriers
Introverts and "Dumb" Questions: Garima stresses that no question is dumb. Asking a question as an attendee helps the speaker remember you. She encourages introverts to treat networking like an interview—you have to speak up to be noticed [31:33].

Safety Tip: She advises girls to be cautious with their personal information; keep phone numbers hidden on LinkedIn and only provide them if a connection feels genuine after checking their profile [29:45].

6. Recommended Programs & Resources
MLSA (Microsoft Learn Student Ambassador): Highly recommended for connecting with top-tier peers [15:33].

GitHub Campus Expert: A prestigious program for community leaders [16:09].

Web3 Learning: She suggests the Alchemy University course for those wanting to start in Web3 development [19:54].

Communities to Follow: Girls in Tech, Azure Developer Community, and CNCG (Cloud Native Computing Group) [18:09].

The session concludes with an interactive Q&A and a reminder that networking is a skill built over time through consistency and providing value to others.

Title: Entrepneurship & Strategic Thinking for Success
Session Summary:
The video titled "Entrepreneurship & Strategic Thinking," featuring Nidhi Mehta (Founder of Self Achievers and a TEDx speaker), provides a comprehensive guide for women looking to venture into the startup world. It was part of the "EmpowerHer" event organized by the Girls Leading Tech community.

Below is a detailed summary of the key insights and strategic advice shared:

1. Entrepreneurship as a Career for Women
Nidhi emphasizes that entrepreneurship is uniquely suited for women because it offers flexibility and lifelong professional continuity [15:14].

Adaptability: She shares her personal journey of moving between three countries and two continents over 23 years. Entrepreneurship allowed her to restart her career each time without a gap [14:56].

Juggling Roles: It provides the flexibility to scale up or down during major life transitions, such as motherhood or caring for aging parents, without sacrificing one's identity or career [16:56].

Financial Independence: Beyond just making money, Nidhi highlights that financial freedom is about empowerment and confidence, allowing women to live life on their own terms [20:46].

2. Strategic Thinking and Business Ideation
Nidhi provides a practical framework for evaluating and launching a business idea:

The Idea Filter: Don’t just dive into an idea blindly. Research the competition and identify a Value Proposition that makes your brand unique [21:20].

The "Rule of Three": If you can list at least three unique things your product does that competitors don't, you have a viable idea [31:02].

Profitability Over Investment: Interestingly, Nidhi started her first business with just $100 and her second with 50,000 Rupees from personal savings [28:11]. She advocates for creating a profitable model and plowing that money back into the business rather than seeking external investment immediately [28:37].

3. Overcoming Psychological Barriers
A significant portion of the session addresses the mental challenges women face:

Dealing with "Pressure": She views being female as a "privilege" because women are naturally wired to manage multiple tasks with focus. She advises "blocking the noise" from those who try to bring you down due to their own insecurities [25:46].

Redefining Failure: Nidhi suggests removing the word "failure" from your dictionary. Instead, view it as an outcome that didn't meet expectations, which still provides a foundation of learning for the next attempt [36:29].

Self-Doubt: She admits that even after 23 years, she still feels self-doubt when learning new skills (like crochet). The remedy is deliberate practice, which eventually builds the confidence to overcome that doubt [34:54].

4. Personal Growth and Lifelong Learning
Connecting the Dots: Nidhi shares a story about learning dance from a young Shahid Kapoor years before she ever thought of it as a career [56:01]. She emphasizes that no learning is ever wasted; skills acquired today often become the foundation for a business years later [56:22].

Prioritization: To be successful, one must learn to prioritize "hard work over reels." She notes that while others might be watching Netflix, an entrepreneur should be working on their business strategy or upskilling [45:13].

Networking and Tribe: Building a supportive community (a "tribe") is essential. She encourages being active on LinkedIn and reaching out to people you admire for a simple coffee chat to foster connections [51:16].

5. Advice for Parents and Family Dynamics
Addressing the common issue of parental skepticism, Nidhi advises not to fight with parents but to recognize that their concern often comes from a place of protection [42:01]. Instead, use their critiques to calculate risks more effectively and prove the business's viability through results [42:46].

Conclusion:
The video serves as a motivational roadmap, encouraging young women to embrace their unique creative skills—whether it's dance, coding, or handicrafts—and marry them with a strong business strategy to achieve long-term fulfillment and success.

Title: How to deal with FOMO, Burnout & Imposter Syndrome
Session Summary:
The video, titled "How to Deal with FOMO, Burnout & Impostor Syndrome," features speaker Ramesh Rajini, who shares her extensive corporate and community-building experience to help young professionals navigate the emotional challenges of the modern tech world.

1. Understanding the Core Issues
The speaker begins by defining three common psychological hurdles that often go undiscussed in technical forums:

FOMO (Fear of Missing Out): The anxiety that others are having more success or better experiences, often triggered by social media platforms like LinkedIn and Instagram [15:59].

Burnout: A state of emotional and physical exhaustion caused by juggling multiple responsibilities, such as college assignments, extracurriculars, and personal life [08:50].

Imposter Syndrome: Feeling like your achievements are due to luck rather than your own skills, leading to constant self-doubt [09:08].

2. The Framework for Navigating Needs
Rajini uses Maslow’s Hierarchy of Needs to explain why these feelings occur:

Basic Needs: Food, clothing, and shelter are often provided by parents for students [11:37].

Security & Social Needs: Once basic needs are met, the fear of not getting a job or the desire to fit into a peer group (social needs) creates stress [11:55].

Purpose: She emphasizes that understanding your "Purpose of Life" is the key to rising above FOMO. When your goal is clear, what others post on social media matters less [16:11].

3. The "Bottle Catalyst" Analogy
A central visual metaphor in the video involves draining water from a bottle to represent emotional release [16:42]:

Natural Drain: Takes the longest.

Shaking the Bottle: Speeds up the process slightly.

Using a Catalyst (a straw): The fastest way.

The Lesson: The "bottle" is the person, and the "water" is the buildup of stress. To avoid bursting, you must find a catalyst—like a mentor, a community, or a trusted friend—to help drain that pressure quickly [19:10].

4. Strategic Solutions & Catalysts
Rajini offers several practical tools for managing these feelings:

Accountability Partners: Find someone to keep you on track so tasks don't just "run in your brain," which causes more exhaustion than the work itself [23:32].

The Power of "No": Practice an "assertive, polite no." Taking too much on your plate is a primary cause of burnout [26:11].

Decision Matrix: When stuck in "Decision Paralysis," write down a 2x2 matrix comparing your current state vs. a future state (considering both the pros and cons of each) [32:25].

The "I Am Remarkable" Exercise: A practice of acknowledging your accomplishments out loud. She notes that "accomplishments don't speak for themselves; you have to speak for them" [35:51].

5. The "Three Circles" of Influence
To reduce FOMO, she suggests focusing only on the people who truly matter [33:03]:

Circle 1: Your immediate family and mentors.

Circle 2: Your close colleagues or managers.

Circle 3: Your reporting line or close network.

Anything beyond these circles (the 4th circle) should not be a source of concern or comparison [34:12].

Conclusion
The speaker concludes by reminding the audience that their worth is not defined by their output [35:38]. Failing at a project or feeling the need to give up on a task is part of the journey, provided you reflect on your goals and maintain a supportive community.

Title: How Leadership & Public Speaking Build Your Confidence
Session Summary:
In this detailed presentation titled "How Leadership & Public Speaking Build Your Confidence," Shilpi Mitra, a lead at Microsoft with over 18 years of experience, shares her journey and practical strategies for personal and professional growth.

1. Defining Confidence
Shilpi emphasizes that confidence is not an innate trait but something built "brick by brick" through practice [11:25].

Key Definition: It is the belief in one’s ability and judgment [11:52].

Actionable Confidence: It manifests as asking questions in a session or stepping up for an unknown project [12:01].

The Comparison Trap: True confidence isn't thinking you are better than everyone; it’s walking into a room without needing to compare yourself to anyone at all [46:44].

2. The Role of Leadership
Leadership is framed as a responsibility to achieve goals for a community rather than just a title [17:00].

Core Traits: Effective leaders exhibit empathy, decisiveness, and effective communication [20:25].

Opportunities for Growth: Students are encouraged to join student bodies, lead hackathons, or volunteer for projects to practice these skills in a "psychologically safe space" [27:12].

Mentorship: Shilpi highlights that you can have multiple mentors for different traits and encourages seeking them through platforms like LinkedIn or Topmate [28:14].

3. Mastering Public Speaking
Shilpi identifies public speaking as one of the hardest but most essential skills for advocating for one's ideas [29:15].

The "Murphy’s Law" Story: She shares a vulnerable story about a high-stakes presentation at Adobe Max in front of 5,000 people where her demo failed and she "froze." She learned that engaging the audience is more important than a perfect technical demo [31:46].

Techniques for Anxiety:

Deep Breathing: Alleviates immediate physical anxiety [37:32].

The Eye Contact Trick: Look at the farthest corners of the room rather than directly into people's eyes if you feel intimidated [38:56].

Q-Cards: Keep keywords written down to avoid losing your "chain of thought" [38:18].

AI Tools: Use PowerPoint’s "Speaker Coach" to track filler words and pacing [40:24].

4. Personal Growth and Career Advancement
Confidence directly influences career trajectory by enabling one to take risks and say "yes" to opportunities [43:04].

Growth Mindset: Continuous learning is the foundation of confidence. When your competence (skill level) increases, your confidence naturally follows [44:12].

Action Plan: She challenges the audience to write down one specific action they will take within the next month to build their confidence [47:33].

Recommended Resources [48:55]
Books: * The Art of Public Speaking by Dale Carnegie

Talk Like TED by Carmine Gallo

Presence by Amy Cuddy

My Life in Full by Indra Nooyi

Lean In by Sheryl Sandberg

Title: Unlock Your hidden potential and live life to the fullest
Session Summary:
This 53-minute video features a guest lecture by Dr. Naga Swathi, a veteran SAP strategist, author, and energy transformation coach, as part of the #5 EMpowerHer series. The session focuses on strategies to overcome mental blocks, identify personal strengths, and achieve a fulfilling life through "makeovers."

1. Defining and Identifying Potential
Dr. Swathi begins by redefining "potential" as the internal strength, courage, and stamina required to achieve targets [03:22]. She emphasizes that everyone has a hidden talent—whether it's coding, mathematics, or communication—but it is often obscured by societal pressure and the pursuit of external validation, such as high grades [04:16].

To help viewers find their true calling, she proposes four reflective questions:

What is the one thing you can do without the limit of time and effort (what brings you joy)? [05:37]

Is there a possibility of making this a source of income? [06:39]

What can you be or do to turn this passion into a profession? [07:15]

What are your true skills versus what you "truly desire"? [08:41]

2. Breaking the "Invisible Cages"
A core theme is the "four makeovers" needed to unlock potential. Dr. Swathi explains that humans are often trapped in "invisible cages" built by:

Childhood Conditioning: She shares a story of an elephant that stays tied to a small stake because it was conditioned as a calf to believe it couldn't move [10:58].

Subconscious Scripts: She recounts personal anecdotes where her mother’s comments about always being "second" created a subconscious block that followed her into her professional career [12:10].

Fear of Success/Failure: Many people avoid the "limelight" because they fear the added expectations that come with being a high achiever [16:49].

3. Practical Tools for Personal Growth
Dr. Swathi offers several actionable techniques to manage energy and mindset:

Journaling: Described as the "biggest tool," journaling helps bring subconscious "garbage" to light [19:58].

Mind Mapping: Used to gain clarity on complex ideas by visualizing resources, finances, and pros/cons [24:27].

The 3 Questions for Ideas: Always ask Why (is it needed?), What (am I doing?), and How (will I do it?) [29:17].

Energy Management: Instead of "work-life balance," she advocates for "Work-Life Integration." She suggests taking 1–2 minute "breathing breaks" every hour to clear mental "traffic" [37:32].

4. Q&A and Overcoming Social Pressures
During the Q&A session, she addresses common concerns for women in tech:

Parental Expectations: She suggests shifting perspective to view parental concern as a protective "shield" rather than a projection of pressure [33:59].

Imposter Syndrome: She warns against the anxiety of "falling behind" others. She encourages viewers to nurture themselves and recognize that resting is part of the growth process [46:16].

Prioritization: To avoid burnout from signing up for too many initiatives, she advises choosing the one thing that will provide the most value and focusing on it deeply [48:13].

Conclusion: The talk concludes with a call to action to practice gratitude and a 4-step process for problem-solving: Question, Journal, Visualize, and Affirm [39:27].

Title: Discussion on Mental Health
Session Summary:
This video features Madhu Sathvik, a yoga trainer and life coach, discussing holistic approaches to mental health and wellness. She emphasizes that mental well-being can be achieved naturally through lifestyle changes, nutrition, and mindfulness.

Core Pillars of Mental Wellness
The Gut-Brain Connection: Madhu explains that food is not just for physical health but directly impacts mental states [04:01]. She advocates for Sattvic food—fresh, wholesome, and home-cooked meals—which boosts energy and focus, whereas processed junk food can lead to acidity and a cluttered mind [05:10].

Celebrating Movement: She warns against being "desk-bound" or "couch-bound," noting that "sitting is the new smoking" [08:27]. Physical activity releases "happy hormones" like dopamine and is a quick way to calm overthinking and anxiety [09:16].

Sleep Hygiene and Circadian Rhythm: The speaker highlights how blue light from screens disrupts melatonin and the body's natural biorhythm [13:12]. She suggests shutting off all screens at least 90 minutes before bed to improve hormonal balance and sleep quality [14:49].

Vitamin D and Nature: Sunlight is crucial as it helps produce Vitamin D, which acts as a hormone regulating mood and mental health [17:54].

Psychological & Emotional Well-being
Living with Purpose: Having a clear sense of purpose or a "vision board" provides the excitement needed to wake up positively [20:45].

Relationship with Self: Madhu stresses that the first relationship we have is with ourselves [23:21]. She encourages questioning negative self-talk and affirming one's own value to build confidence [25:45].

Service to Others: Acts of service, even just listening to someone, help shift focus away from one's own problems and foster a sense of gratitude [26:46].

Surrender and Faith: She advises accepting that not everything is within our control [28:33]. Believing in a "benevolent force" or a divine plan can help one be less harsh on themselves during difficult times [30:36].

Practical Exercises & Techniques
Calming Breath: A simple technique where you inhale for a count of 5 and exhale through the mouth for a count of 10 to immediately lower stress [34:44].

Bhramari Pranayama (Bumblebee Breath): A humming practice that creates internal vibrations. These vibrations travel through the nerves and blood vessels to calm the mind and hormones [39:17].

Q&A Highlights
Dealing with Criticism: To stop being affected by others' words, one must strengthen their internal self-worth through positive affirmations [43:00].

Physical Symptoms of Anxiety: For those who feel anxiety in their stomach or legs, Madhu recommends yoga and stretching to release "negative energy blocks" in the muscles [44:22].

Parental Pressure: She suggests open communication and "venting" feelings to parents or peers rather than suppressing them, which leads to burnout [46:38].

Title: How to become a Codess Cafe Mentee
Speaker Name:
Urvashi Agarwal
Garima Jha
Sweta Pandey
Session Summary:
This video is a detailed panel discussion hosted by the community Girls Leading Tech, featuring three mentees (Urvashi, Garima, and Suvarna) who share their experiences and advice on how to successfully apply to and benefit from Codess.Cafe, a prominent mentorship platform for women in tech.

Introduction and Panelist Journeys
The session begins with the panelists sharing how they discovered the community, primarily through LinkedIn networking [04:46].

Suvarna: A second-year student who joined the 11th cohort and later the Machine Learning (ML) cohort. She emphasized the value of the community's hackathons and the guidance of mentors like Arsh Goyal [05:52].

Urvashi: Currently part of the Open Source cohort, she focused on how the community helped her transition from being a spectator on LinkedIn to an active contributor in open-source projects [07:06].

Garima: A summer intern at DRDO, she highlighted that she joined Codess.Cafe to find the guidance she was missing in her career, which eventually led her to dream placements and networking opportunities [07:43].

The Application and Selection Process
The core of the video addresses the specific criteria needed to get accepted into a cohort:

Core Parameters: Selection is heavily based on a candidate's LinkedIn profile (activity and connections) and GitHub profile (quality of projects) [08:49].

The "Hunger" Factor: Garima notes that the selection committee looks for "hunger" and genuine determination in the application [10:04].

Essay Questions: These are often more critical than the resume itself. Applicants must be authentic, explaining exactly what they expect from the program and why they are willing to dedicate their time [11:39].

Resumes for Freshers: For first-year students with limited experience, the panelists suggest including basic projects, certifications (even introductory ones like Linux or Infosys), and school-level leadership roles [14:14].

Cohort Structures
The video clarifies the difference between various types of programs within Codess.Cafe:

Generic Cohort: Focuses on personalized mentorship (usually one mentor for 10 girls), resume building, and general career guidance [27:59].

Specialized Cohorts (ML & Open Source): These are technical and curriculum-based. For example, the ML cohort includes sessions on Python, AI/ML theory, quizzes, and a final capstone project [37:38].

Community Groups: Beyond direct mentorship, there are large WhatsApp groups with over 1,000 members where daily job opportunities and resources are shared [28:06].

Practical Tips for Applicants
Activity as Experience: If you lack professional experience, the host suggests listing "Active Contributor" roles in tech communities (like Girls Leading Tech) on your resume to show engagement [31:51].

GPA Requirements: While a high CGPA (around 8.0) is helpful due to high competition, students with lower scores are still encouraged to apply if their projects and essays are strong [33:17].

Mentorship Longevity: Mentors often provide guidance far beyond the official cohort timeline, assisting with LinkedIn chats and referrals long after the sessions end [36:25].

Community Updates
The host, Manik, reveals a "secret" project: the community is building its own centralized website to consolidate scattered resources like scholarships, internships, and mentorships into one place [20:55].

The session concludes with a Q&A where panelists encourage introverted students to build confidence by participating in these live sessions [19:05].

Title: How to find the best events happening around you
Session Summary:
The video "How to find the Events Happening around you?" by the channel Girls Leading Tech is a comprehensive guide and interactive session led by Manik. It focuses on the importance of networking, how to discover tech events, and strategies for making the most out of them.

1. The Importance of Networking and Events
Real-world Impact: A guest named Katherine shares her experience attending a marketing event despite being an engineering student. This led to her connecting with founders and senior engineers, eventually securing an internship opportunity [01:28].

Personal Growth: Manik emphasizes that events are about making friends, not just professional "networking." These connections can lead to jobs, mentorship, and co-founders [29:15].

Overcoming Barriers: Manik encourages those who are shy to step out of their comfort zones, sharing how she overcame her own fear of being seen in meetings to become a regular speaker and community leader [03:45].

2. How to Evaluate an Event
Before registering, Manik suggests researching specific criteria to ensure the event is worth your time and energy [08:15]:

Sponsors: Look at the companies supporting the event (e.g., Google, Alchemy) to gauge the quality and potential career connections [18:06].

Agenda: Check if the topics align with your interests (AI, Blockchain, Soft Skills) [08:38].

Venue: Is it a professional hotel, a college, or a co-working space? The venue often reflects the event's scale [08:32].

Speakers/Mentors: Research them on LinkedIn beforehand. This allows you to prepare specific questions, making your interactions more meaningful than a generic "hello" [11:50].

3. Platforms to Find Events
Manik shares a curated list of platforms to track upcoming opportunities [32:46]:

Hackathons: MLH, Devfolio, and Devpost [33:10].

Tech-Specific: Luma (great for AI/ML and Web3 events), Kaggle (for Data Science), and ETH Global (for Blockchain) [45:00].

Community Groups: Google Developer Groups (GDG), Women Techmakers (WTM), and Meetup.com [42:06].

Flagship Events: Keep an eye on annual conferences from giants like Google (Google I/O), Microsoft, Canva, and GitHub [15:24].

4. Strategies for Getting Selected
For "flagship" or "premium" events with limited seating, Manik suggests:

Apply Early: Many organizers review applications on a rolling basis; once spots are filled, late applications are ignored [21:47].

Direct Outreach: Find the organizer on LinkedIn and send a DM expressing your genuine passion. Offer to contribute—for example, by writing an article or creating a vlog about the event [23:01].

Build Proof of Work: Having a strong LinkedIn or GitHub profile acts as your "proof" that you are a worthy attendee [25:34].

5. Safety and Practical Tips
Safety for Women: Especially for solo travelers, check the timings, the safety of the locality, and transportation accessibility [30:42].

Travel Strategy: If you live in a small city (like Manik or some attendees), be selective. Virtual events are a great way to build an international network without the exhaustion of travel [10:34].

Paid vs. Free: While free events are accessible, paid events often provide a "higher quality" of audience because they filter for genuine interest. However, for students, Manik suggests looking for volunteering opportunities to attend these for free [59:13].

6. Leveraging the Event (Post-Event)
Capture Memories: Take photos with speakers and attendees [01:09:49].

The Follow-Up: Don't let the connection die. Message your new contacts a few days later or set up a "coffee chat" via Google Meet to deepen the relationship [01:10:11].

Public Sharing: Post about what you learned on LinkedIn to build your own brand and "proof of work" [01:10:04].

The session concludes with a reminder that while professional growth is vital, one should not neglect health and family for the "virtual world" [01:11:01].

Title: How to Crack Google
Session Summary:
In this video, Deeksha Pandey, an SDE III at Google, shares her detailed journey from a Tier-3 college to cracking top tech companies like Microsoft and Google.

Educational Background and Early Challenges
College Environment: Deeksha attended a state government college (BIET Jhansi) after not getting her preferred branch in NITs [01:44]. She notes that the college had a poor coding culture initially but offered flexibility with attendance, allowing her time for self-study [02:32].

Starting Point: She officially began her coding journey on May 1, 2020, during the COVID-19 lockdown, after finally arranging a laptop [03:31].

The Learning Roadmap
C++ & DSA: She spent about a month learning C++ through GeeksforGeeks and HackerRank [03:52]. For DSA, she followed the book Data Structures and Algorithms Made Easy by Narasimha Karumanchi [04:35].

Implementation Strategy: A key takeaway she emphasizes is implementing every data structure and algorithm from scratch rather than just solving word problems [05:05].

Competitive Programming (CP): Knowing that top companies wouldn't visit her campus, she started CP to build a strong profile [06:11]. Her routine involved 5-6 hours of DSA, 1-2 hours of web development, and 3-4 hours of CP daily [06:53].

Career Success & Job Hunt
The "Off-Campus" Struggle: She applied to many companies and faced numerous rejections for 7-8 months before landing her first offer from Infosys through HackWithInfy [07:31].

Microsoft & Google: She secured a Microsoft internship/FT offer via the Fixathon hackathon [08:08]. Shortly after, a Google recruiter reached out to her, leading to her eventual role at Google [08:24].

Total Timeline: It took approximately 1.5 to 2 years of consistent effort to reach her dream companies [11:34].

Key Advice for Aspiring Engineers
Resume Building: For Tier-3 students, she recommends participating in diversity-specific tech events (e.g., Adobe Women in Tech, Google STEP, Flipkart Runway) to stand out [09:22].

Project Metrics: When listing projects, use data points and metrics (e.g., "increased UI visits by 10%") to catch a recruiter's eye [21:19].

Interview Focus: She clarifies that Google interviews are 99% focused on DSA and problem-solving skills rather than specific frameworks or projects [17:18].

Don't Rush: Deeksha critiques "DSA in 30 days" roadmaps, stating that it takes at least 6 months to a year to truly master the concepts [36:49].

Mental Well-being: She reminds students that landing a job is not the "end goal" and encourages them to enjoy college life and avoid burnout [13:38].

Q&A Highlights
Hackathons vs. Internships: She found hackathons more instrumental in her personal journey, as her resume was primarily filled with competition wins [19:04].

Upsolving: To improve at CP, she stresses "upsolving"—re-attempting questions you couldn't solve during a contest after looking at the editorial [23:38].

Pattern Recognition: She suggests maintaining a notebook to categorize problems into "buckets" based on patterns for more efficient revision [34:22].

Title: from topper to AFE Scholar
Session Summary:
This video from the Girls Leading Tech spotlight series features Siddhi Gupta, a first-year Computer Science student at NIT Kurukshetra, who shares her journey from being an academic topper to becoming an Amazon Future Engineer (AFE) Scholar.

Academic Background & Achievements
Academic Excellence: Siddhi has a stellar academic record, scoring 99.4% in Class 10th, 97.6% in Class 12th, and achieving a 99.25 percentile in JEE Mains [00:13].

The AFE Scholarship: She discovered the Amazon Future Engineer scholarship through a senior. The program provides not only financial support but also learning resources and a direct path to an internship at Amazon [02:12].

Selection Process: The selection involved writing a detailed essay about her background and future goals, followed by an interview that focused on her passion for coding and her plans for the scholarship [02:43].

Technical Journey & Consistency
Early Start: Siddhi began coding in 9th grade with Python, HTML, and CSS [04:53].

College Preparation: Before even starting her first semester, she proactively completed nearly half of her Data Structures and Algorithms (DSA) curriculum using YouTube resources like Striver [05:31].

Current Tech Stack: By the end of her first year, she had completed a significant amount of DSA (300-400 questions) and moved into Web Development, learning JavaScript, React, and Backend development [06:18].

Daily Routine: She maintains discipline by waking up early to solve 3-4 DSA questions daily before attending college classes [12:01].

Success Strategies & Advice
Peer Support: She emphasizes the importance of a like-minded friend circle. Her friends at NIT Kurukshetra participate in coding contests together and hold each other accountable [08:05].

The "Apply First" Mindset: Siddhi’s mantra is to apply for every opportunity (like GSOC or Hackathons) without overthinking whether she is "ready." She believes you become ready through the process [15:51].

Focus on Skills: She notes that while college name (like an NIT) helps, the current market requires strong off-campus application skills. "What matters is your skill; work hard on it" [19:39].

Consistency over Intensity: She advises sticking to one topic (like finishing DSA or a specific Web Dev project) rather than constantly switching between subjects, which leads to "tutorial hell" [16:57].

Future Outlook
As an AFE scholar, Siddhi is currently enrolled in a 10-month coding bootcamp. She is preparing for an Online Assessment (OA) in January, which could lead to an internship at Amazon between May and July of her second year [14:15].

Title: How to Crack Intuit
Session Summary:
This video is an extensive mentorship session featuring Akanksha Buchke, a Senior Software Engineer at Intuit, who shares a comprehensive roadmap for students and professionals looking to crack roles at top product-based companies like Intuit, Google, and Microsoft.

The session is structured around a Five-Step Framework designed to take a candidate from a beginner level to being job-ready.

1. The Five-Step Framework [05:10]
Step 1: Master a Programming Language [05:25]

The speaker emphasizes that the specific language is less important than starting.

Java is recommended for those interested in development (Spring Boot).

Python is suggested for beginners or those interested in Data Science/AI.

C++ is preferred for those focused on competitive programming due to execution speed.

Step 2: Practice Data Structures and Algorithms (DSA) [07:11]

Approach: Start with basics (Arrays, Strings), move to intermediate (Stacks, Queues, Linked Lists), and then advanced topics (Trees, DP, Graphs) [10:50].

Consistency: Solve at least 3 questions daily. Over 6 months, this totals ~500 questions [10:04].

Platforms: Use LeetCode (for topic-wise practice) and GeeksforGeeks (for company-specific tags) [10:30].

Step 3: Projects and Internships [21:15]

DSA gets you through coding rounds, but projects/internships are critical for technical interviews.

Recommended domains: Web Development (MERN/Java Spring Boot) or AI/ML [22:42].

Internships: Don't just target Big Tech; startups and government organizations (ISRO, BHEL) provide excellent exposure [23:43].

Step 4: Computer Science Fundamentals [26:10]

Focus on core subjects: OOPS, DBMS, Operating Systems, and Computer Networks.

Prepare for Aptitude and Reasoning to clear initial Online Assessments (OA) [27:19].

Step 5: Resume and LinkedIn Optimization [27:49]

Resume: Use an ATS-friendly template. Avoid photos, multiple colors, or skill-rating bars [35:06].

LinkedIn: Use the "Featured" section for certificates and top coding profiles. Connect with recruiters and employees early, not just when you need a referral [38:12].

2. Intuit-Specific Interview Process [41:03]
Akanksha details the specific hiring workflow at Intuit:

Online Assessment (OA): 4 questions in 90 minutes. Difficulty ranges from LeetCode Medium to Hard [41:11].

Technical Round 1: Primarily focused on DSA (DP, Greedy, Graphs) [41:35].

Technical Round 2: Focused on projects, past experiences, and "Techno-managerial" discussions to check culture fit [42:41].

Final Round: HR discussion and offer release.

3. Key Career Advice & Q&A Highlights
CGPA: Aim for at least 7.0+, though 8.0+ is ideal for staying eligible for all companies [43:51].

Time Management in Interviews: Aim to think and code a solution within 20–25 minutes [14:37].

Handling Failure: If you can't solve a problem after 30 minutes of thinking, watch a tutorial, take notes, and try similar problems later [01:04:04].

System Design: Usually not required for freshers/interns, but you should understand your project's architecture and why you chose specific databases [48:34].

The session concludes with a live Q&A where Akanksha encourages students from Tier-3 colleges to focus heavily on their project quality and networking to overcome the lack of on-campus opportunities [03:42].

Title: she's a We, GHC, SheFi Scholar and HPAIR Delegate
Session Summary:
This video is an interview with Samridhi Gupta, a second-year engineering student who has secured several prestigious scholarships and fellowships. She shares her journey, the application processes for various programs, and advice for women in tech.

1. Key Opportunities & Programs
Samridhi discusses several high-profile programs she has been a part of:

Women Engineers (WE) Program (TalentSprint & Google): [02:00]

A 24-month immersive program for first-year female students.

Selection Process: Includes rounds for Aptitude, Coding (Python/C), English Proficiency, and a Personal Interview [02:42].

Benefits: Access to a supportive community ("WE-booms"), mentorship, and an edge for Google STEP internships [05:18].

HPAIR (Harvard Project for Asian and International Relations): [06:44]

A conference-based program with an application involving situational essay questions and interviews [06:51].
