Creative Highlights
Musical Performances: Gracey performs multiple songs on her guitar throughout the session, showcasing her vocal and instrumental talent [06:03], [33:21].

Interactive Session: The host tracks audience engagement, rewarding top commenters with Canva Premium subscriptions to encourage participation from the "Girls Leading Tech" community [40:17].

Title: quantum curiosity: exploring ideas beyond normal coding
Session Summary:
This video, titled "Quantum Curiosity: Exploring Ideas Beyond Normal Coding," features Ernestine Lerisha, a Quantum and AI Researcher, who provides a detailed introduction to quantum computing and its real-world applications, particularly for those coming from a traditional coding or "hackathon" background.

1. Introduction to Quantum Computing
The session simplifies complex quantum concepts using analogies to make them accessible to non-physicists [03:46].

Bits vs. Qubits: While classical computers use binary bits (0 or 1), quantum computers use qubits, which can exist in both states simultaneously [06:36].

The "Chandelier" Structure: Quantum computers are physically large, complex structures that resemble giant chandeliers and must be kept at extremely low temperatures to remain stable [07:01].

The Maze Analogy: A classical computer navigates a maze by trying every path one by one. In contrast, a quantum computer searches all paths at once, finding the solution exponentially faster [18:53].

2. Core Quantum Concepts
Lerisha explains three fundamental principles that give quantum computing its power:

Superposition: The ability to be in multiple states at once. She compares this to a spinning coin—while in the air, it is neither heads nor tails but a blur of both [14:23].

Entanglement: A phenomenon where two qubits are emotionally or "physically" linked regardless of distance. If you disturb one, the other is instantly affected [25:11].

Measurement & Decoherence: The act of observing or measuring a quantum system causes it to "collapse" into a classical state (0 or 1). This makes hacking quantum systems difficult because any intrusion disturbs the entire system [23:03].

3. Real-World Applications & The Future
The discussion highlights how quantum computing is moving beyond theory into practical use cases:

Cryptography: Quantum computers can break traditional encryption in seconds. However, Quantum Algorithms are being developed to create "quantum-proof" security [19:37].

Drug Discovery: Scientists use quantum simulations to model molecular structures for cancer research and medicine [28:40].

Finance: In stock markets, quantum can predict long-term trends (up to 25 years) far more accurately than classical models [32:36].

The 10-15 Year Horizon: While currently expensive and resource-heavy, Lerisha predicts that within 15-25 years, quantum computers could become as accessible as classical laptops [34:05].

4. Career Advice for Aspiring Researchers
As an incoming intern at Carleton University, Canada, Lerisha shares her journey into the field [01:35]:

Starting Small: You don't need advanced physics to start; basic high school math (probability and statistics) is sufficient [35:34].

Resources: She recommends using simulators like IBM Qiskit, Google, or D-Wave, which offer free execution time for students to experiment with qubits [31:11].

Networking: She gained her internship by cold-emailing professors and collaborating on research publications before applying for formal positions [43:10].

The video concludes with a Q&A session emphasizing that while AI may automate tasks, Quantum Computing will require significant human assistance to implement, meaning it is a field of growing opportunity rather than a threat to jobs [49:33].

Title: building smart, not big: how simple olutions win hackathons
Session Summary:
This video features an inspiring interview and presentation by Manishka Dubey, a 12-year-old founder, robotics innovator, and author. Hosted by "Girls Leading Tech" as a pre-hackathon session for "Code at Christmas," the discussion focuses on her journey, her startup, and her advice for building smart solutions.

1. Manishka’s Background and Journey
Early Start: Manishka began her journey in robotics and coding at the age of five and a half [01:14]. Her interest was sparked after seeing how astronauts work on the International Space Station (ISS) [07:44].

Achievements: By age 12, she has already represented India in international competitions (winning a gold medal in Thailand) [01:38] and is an author of a sci-fi book titled "Tinker to Techie", which chronicles her journey [06:01].

First-Generation Techie: Remarkably, she is the first in her family to enter the tech field; her mother is an artist, and her grandparents come from non-tech backgrounds [06:41].

2. Tinker Techie: The Startup
Manishka is the founder of Tinker Techie, a startup focused on:

Bridging the Gender Gap: She conducts STEM workshops for girls to encourage their participation in robotics [03:18].

Social Impact Products: She has developed child safety bands and products designed to help blind children learn Braille [04:18].

Entrepreneurship: Her startup is incubated at iStart (Rajasthan), and she recently secured her first round of pre-seed funding (3 Lakhs) with zero equity [38:07].

3. Key Presentation: "Building Smart, Not Big"
Manishka shared a presentation aimed at hackathon participants, debunking the myth that a winning project needs to be complex or feature-heavy [10:21].

Smart Execution: Reality is that judges reward smart execution over "big" ideas [10:44].

The Power of MVP (Minimum Viable Product): She defines an MVP as the simplest working version of an idea that proves the core concept [13:14].

Core Advice: * Focus on one core function rather than trying to build everything at once [11:22].

Embrace imperfection; your first prototype doesn't have to look fancy, it just needs to work [12:06].

Clarity is more powerful than complexity in the eyes of judges [14:37].

4. Lifestyle and Education
Virtual Schooling: Manishka attends 21K School, an online school that allows her to balance her studies with her travels and startup work [25:09]. She enjoys the flexibility and the ability to connect with students globally [26:51].

Future Goals: She plans to attend MIT for her higher education [28:30] and sees herself as a successful entrepreneur in five years [05:08].

Balance: Despite her professional achievements, she emphasizes the importance of being a "social person," watching cartoons, playing with her brother, and spending quality time with family [24:47].

5. Closing Message
Her message to girls and fellow students is to follow your passion and give 100% to whatever you do [05:15]. She encourages everyone to reach out to her via LinkedIn for mentorship or support [09:15].

Title: from idea to product: turn a random idea into a real app
Session Summary:
The video, titled "From Idea to Product: Turn a Random Idea into a Real App using Vibe Coding," features Shreya Sethi (Founder of XA Vision Apps) demonstrating how to build a functional web application rapidly using AI-driven "Vibe Coding."

Core Concept: What is Vibe Coding?
"Vibe Coding" is defined as building software using natural language to "vibe" with AI tools. Instead of writing manual code, the developer acts as a director, giving high-level instructions to AI assistants that generate and refine the application in real-time [07:20].

Step-by-Step Workflow
Shreya outlines a structured 4-step process to move from a concept to a live product:

Product Requirement Document (PRD): * Before touching any code, create a document defining who the app is for, what problem it solves, and its core features.

For the workshop demo, Shreya builds "Mentor Hunt," an app that helps students book calls with working professionals [12:18].

AI-Generated Prompts (using Chat GPT):

Instead of giving a "messy" prompt to the app builder, Shreya feeds the PRD into ChatGPT to generate a detailed, structured prompt for the builder tool [19:40].

This detailed prompt includes layout, user flow, and functional requirements [21:02].

Building the App (using Lovable.ai):

Shreya uses Lovable.ai to build the app from the prompt.

Features Integrated:

Student Dashboard: To view and book mentors [38:30].

Mentor Dashboard: For professionals to list their services and set availability [36:39].

Database (Supabase): Integrated to store mentor and booking data [40:21].

Booking Tool (Calendly): Used as a ready-made external tool for scheduling meetings [37:14].

Testing and Publishing:

Shreya demonstrates real-time debugging: if a feature isn't working, she simply tells the AI, "Solve this," and the tool fixes the code itself [52:09].

The final app is published with a live URL, and its security settings are scanned and updated automatically [01:11:00].

Key Takeaways and Tips
Speed is Key: For hackathons (like the one discussed in the video), don't build everything from scratch—use existing tools like Calendly and Supabase to save time [38:08].

Version Control: Shreya recommends using GitHub with Vibe Coding to manage "commits." This allows you to revert to a previous version if an AI change "ruins everything" [01:08:05].

Minimal Viable Product (MVP): For a 4-hour hackathon, focus on 2-3 core features rather than a full-scale app [01:04:12].

Final App Result: A live, functional application called "Mentor Hunt" where students can sign up, search for mentors, and book calls—all built in about 20-30 minutes of real-time "vibe coding" [39:32].

Title: from idea to impact - designing ai experiences that users trust
Session Summary:
This video, titled "From Idea to Impact: Designing AI Experiences That Users Trust," features Aprajita, a Front-end Architect at Mycom with over 17 years of experience. She provides a comprehensive guide for developers and students on how to integrate AI into products meaningfully, focusing on usability, ethics, and user trust.

1. The Reality of AI Integration Today
Aprajita highlights common "pitfalls" seen in modern development and hackathons:

"AI Slapping": Developers often force AI into products where it isn't necessary just to look "cool" or attract judges [05:48].

Idea-Driven vs. Impact-Driven: Instead of asking "What can I build with AI?", developers should ask "I have a problem; can AI help solve it?" [08:10].

Hidden Costs: AI integration isn't free; it comes with API costs and performance trade-offs that must be justified [07:07].

2. Building User Trust
A central theme of the session is that AI usability is rooted in trust [15:58]. Key strategies include:

Transparency: Never hide the logic from the user. If AI makes a decision (like a bank transaction), the user needs to understand why [16:04].

Disclaimers: Especially in critical fields like medicine, always state that content is AI-generated and not 100% accurate [18:44].

Human-in-the-loop: AI should suggest, not dictate. Use terms like "suggested" or "likely" rather than being definitive [19:45].

Graceful Failure: Always design a "fallback" or error state for when the AI API is down or credits run out [20:51].

3. Beyond Conversational UI
Aprajita argues that AI is not just about chatbots. She demonstrates several "Smart UI" use cases using the Syncfusion library [28:12]:

Smart Paste: Automatically filling out complex forms from a block of natural language text [29:20].

Anomaly Detection: Identifying patterns or outliers in massive datasets (e.g., millions of telecom records) without manual algorithms [30:54].

Visual Generation: Using AI to generate JSON structures that automatically render flowcharts or maps [31:46].

Query Builders: Allowing users to filter databases using natural language instead of complex SQL syntax [32:32].

4. Career and Ethical Advice for Students
During the Q&A, she addresses the future of tech careers:

AI as an Assistant, Not a Replacement: She emphasizes that developers must understand the underlying systems (like React or Angular) to effectively debug AI-generated code, which can often be "garbage" if not prompted correctly [36:00].

Environmental Impact: She encourages students to be "sustainable" by only using AI when necessary, as it consumes significant power and computing resources [34:24].

The "Unicorn" Reality Check: A product that crashes when an API is down is just a "hackathon demo," not a scalable production system or a potential unicorn startup [27:55].

5. Closing Thoughts
The session concludes with the message that while AI is advancing, mathematical absolutes suggest it may never fully replicate human intelligence [40:06]. The goal for future developers is to maintain their thinking ability while leveraging AI to increase productivity [35:42].

Title: building for social good - dpi and dpg
Session Summary:
This detailed summary covers the "Building for Social Good" session hosted by Girls Leading Tech, featuring Ajitha, the Program Coordinator at Code4GovTech (C4GT). The video serves as an educational deep dive into how technology can be leveraged for social impact through open-source initiatives.

Core Concepts: DPGs and DPIs
Ajitha begins by explaining the foundational pillars of building for social good:

Sustainable Development Goals (SDGs): Established by the UN in 2015, these 17 "Global Goals" aim to eliminate poverty, protect the planet, and ensure prosperity by 2030.

Open Source Software (OSS): Software with source code accessible for anyone to inspect, modify, and enhance. While often free for social causes, some licensing fees may apply for commercial use. Examples include Linux, MongoDB, and Wikipedia.

Digital Public Goods (DPGs): These are open-source software, data, or AI models that specifically address SDGs. To be officially recognized as a DPG, a product must be vetted by the Digital Public Goods Alliance (DPGA) based on criteria like interoperability and open standards.

Digital Public Infrastructure (DPI): Technology built at a "population scale" typically managed or initiated by governments. Examples include Aadhaar (identity) and UPI (payments). Unlike DPGs, DPIs do not necessarily have to be open-source.

The Role of Code4GovTech (C4GT)
Ajitha explains that DPGs often face challenges with long-term maintenance and community building due to limited funding. Code4GovTech acts as a bridge to solve this:

The Ecosystem: C4GT connects organizations (governments, NGOs, DPG projects) with a talent pipeline of students and professionals.

Partner Organizations: They work with entities like MOSIP (modular open-source identity platform), National Health Authority (NHA), and Planet Read (literacy through subtitling).

Flagship Opportunities for Students
The video highlights two main ways for students to get involved and get paid for their contributions:

Dedicated Mentoring Program (DMP):

This is a flagship 3-month summer program similar to Google Summer of Code (GSoC).

Perks: Selected contributors receive 1:1 mentorship and a stipend of ₹1 Lakh.

Timeline: Applications typically open around March.

Open Community:

An ongoing initiative where students can solve "tickets" or issues of varying complexity.

It features a leaderboard system, and some specific tickets also carry monetary rewards (e.g., ₹20,000 for shorter projects).

Application & Selection Process
To succeed in joining the DMP, Ajitha provides the following advice:

Prior Contribution: Start making open-source contributions early to understand Git workflows.

Proposal Quality: When applications open, students must submit a detailed proposal on how they will solve a specific project over three months. Note: Using AI/ChatGPT for proposals is highly discouraged as they are rigorously scrutinized.

Rigorous Screening: The process involves proposal shortlisting followed by interviews with the host organizations.

Pro Tip: Research previous projects on the C4GT Wiki or GitHub to gain an edge before the application cycle begins.

The session concludes with an encouraging note on the importance of using tech skills for the "larger public good" rather than just focusing on financial gain.

Title: ubser she++ mentorship session 2026
how to crack linkedin coachin 2026
Session Summary:
This video is a comprehensive guide on how to crack the LinkedIn CoachIn program and secure a subsequent internship at LinkedIn. The discussion features two former mentees, Garima and Kritika, who share their end-to-end journey from application to final interviews.

1. Program Overview & Eligibility
The LinkedIn CoachIn program is a 6-month mentorship initiative designed specifically for second-year female engineering students [01:11].

Goal: To provide technical mentorship, problem-solving skills, and industry readiness [01:19].

Eligibility: Must be a female student in her 2nd year (4-year course) or 3rd year (5-year course) with a CGPA > 7.0 [02:42].

Perks: Personalized 1-on-1 mentorship from LinkedIn employees, mock interviews, DSA sessions, and a chance to win prizes like iPads or MacBooks in a program-end hackathon [02:20], [30:33].

2. Selection Process for Mentorship
The selection into the CoachIn program consists of three primary stages:

Application & LinkedIn Profile: Your LinkedIn profile acts as your resume [03:02]. It should be fully optimized with an "About" section, projects, and a professional photo [03:24].

Online Assessment (OA): This is the most significant filtering round where approximately 70-80% of candidates are eliminated [16:40]. It typically features 3 easy-to-medium LeetCode-style problems (Arrays, Graphs, Priority Queues) [04:57], [06:05].

Telephonic Interview: A 10–20 minute general discussion about your background, projects, coding profile, and your motivation for joining the program [17:47], [18:14].

3. Preparation Resources & Tips
The speakers recommend focusing on fundamental Data Structures and Algorithms (DSA):

Resources: Striver’s SDE Sheet (solving ~70% is sufficient), LeetCode contests, and the LeetCode 150 Interview study plan [08:19], [08:55].

Key Topics: Arrays, Strings, Graphs (BFS), Trees, and Dynamic Programming (DP) [07:31], [40:06].

Optimization: In the OA, passing test cases isn't enough; the optimality of your code (time and space complexity) and the speed of completion are critical factors [06:51], [12:47].

4. The Internship Opportunity
All students who successfully complete the mentorship program receive a direct link to the internship OA, bypassing the general resume screening [38:09].

Internship Rounds: Includes a Medium-Hard OA, a Technical Interview (40 mins, 2 DSA questions), and a Host Manager round (managerial/team-fit focus) [44:23], [46:29].

The Internship: Usually takes place in Bangalore during the summer after the 3rd year [48:47]. It offers a high stipend (approx. 1.5–2 Lakhs per month) and a path to a Full-Time Offer (PPO) [48:55], [50:50].

5. The Mentorship Experience
Once selected, mentees engage in:

Monthly DSA Sessions: Guided paths for different topics [23:06].

1-on-1 Mentorship: Bi-weekly meetings with a dedicated LinkedIn mentor for career guidance and mock interviews [23:11].

GenAI Hackathon: A team project (with members from different colleges) to build a Generative AI solution for LinkedIn [23:26], [29:14].

Title: how technology students can stay relevent in the ai era
Session Summary:
This video features Bhagyasrie M. Masorkar, a Digital Transformation Leader with over 20 years of experience, discussing how students and professionals can remain relevant and resilient in the age of Artificial Intelligence.

Core Philosophy: AI as a Collaborator, Not a Threat
The central theme of the session is that AI is not here to replace humans but to complement and collaborate with them [05:41]. Bhagyasrie emphasizes that while machines can automate tasks, they cannot replace human intuition, strategy, or emotional intelligence [06:10].

Key Discussion Points
1. Transformation Across Industries [07:00]
Bhagyasrie details how AI is currently reshaping various sectors:

Healthcare: Improving diagnosis accuracy and speeding up research by synthesizing massive amounts of data [07:10].

Banking: Enhancing fraud detection and security in a highly regulated digital environment [07:46].

Retail: Using personal recommendations (like Amazon) to transform the customer experience and ease of transactions [09:18].

Education: Enabling lifelong, parallel learning through visual and interactive online platforms [08:50].

2. Human Skills for the AI Era [12:03]
She identifies critical human skills that AI cannot replicate, which she calls "our authority":

Critical Thinking: Determining the strategic use and desired outcomes of AI-generated data [12:43].

Emotional Intelligence: Navigating complex human interactions and leadership [13:05].

Communication & Storytelling: Clearly articulating ideas and problem-solving journeys [23:11].

Ethical Judgment: Managing AI hallucinations and ensuring compliance in regulated industries [25:04].

3. The Power of Campus Innovation [15:40]
Bhagyasrie encourages students to look beyond textbooks, noting that major revolutions often start on college campuses. She cites famous examples of campus-born innovations:

Meta (Facebook): Started as Mark Zuckerberg’s curiosity to connect students [28:39].

Dropbox: Created by Drew Houston to solve the personal "pain point" of forgetting USB drives [31:24].

Google: Began as a PhD research project at Stanford [32:30].

4. Strategies for Continuous Learning [14:43]
To stay relevant, she suggests:

Leveraging Platforms: Using Coursera, Udemy, and open-source communities to learn at your own pace [16:40].

Active Participation: Engaging in hackathons and coding competitions to experiment and build confidence [17:35].

Building Projects: Starting with small projects like AI chatbots or data visualization tools to understand the technology [19:46].

Key Takeaways for Students [38:19]
Embrace Adaptability: Maintain openness toward new technologies instead of fearing job displacement.

Focus on Core Skills: When "AI-proofing" a resume, emphasize the specific problem-solving skills and core technical competencies required for the role [39:10].

Network Strategically: Use tech forums and hackathons to connect with peers and senior industry leaders [18:16].

Title: the rise of women leadershio in data & AI: Opportunities for the next generation
Session Summary:
This video features an interview with Su Jella, a distinguished digital data and AI strategist, discussing the evolving landscape of women’s leadership in technology. The conversation focuses on how diversity influences AI development, strategies for career growth, and the human skills that remain essential in an automated world.

1. Mitigating Bias in AI through Diverse Leadership
Su Jella emphasizes that having women in leadership is critical for creating fair AI models:

Diverse Perspectives: Women and other diverse groups provide the necessary "guardrails" to test models more deeply, identifying inaccuracies that a homogenous team might miss [02:40].

Edge Case Identification: Diverse mindsets help in understanding how different population segments (e.g., binary vs. non-binary) are treated by algorithms, leading to more inclusive data curation [03:10].

Human in the Loop: She advocates for the "augmentation of human and AI," ensuring humans remain involved in decision-making to prevent reliance on narrow or biased datasets [04:33].

2. Navigating the Threat of Automation
Addressing concerns that entry-level roles (often held by women) are being automated out:

Continuous Learning: Su argues that your strength lies in constant evolution. Even as AI handles coding, "decoding"—understanding where things went wrong, resolving cyber issues, and identifying bad actors—will always require human expertise [10:17].

New Job Creation: She uses the evolution of the telephone as an analogy: manual dialing jobs vanished, but mobile technology created hundreds of new roles in app development, security, and hardware [13:03].

3. Essential "Human-Centric" Skills
In the AI era, Su identifies three non-technical skills that provide a competitive edge [17:09]:

Storytelling & Communication: The ability to deliver a narrative that builds trust and resonates with an audience.

Intuition & Curiosity: Being able to recognize patterns and "golden nuggets" in data that AI might synthesize but cannot fully interpret in a human context.

Human Awareness: Bringing a "natural human narrative" to the table so the brand remains trustworthy and relatable [18:29].

4. Career Growth: Networking, Mentors, and Sponsors
Su provides a clear distinction between the types of support a professional needs [29:04]:

Mentors: Act as coaches and guides who help you understand your gaps and strengthen your assets.

Sponsors: Advocates within your organization or industry who actively introduce you to opportunities and "vouch" for your delivery.

Visibility: She encourages "amplifying your voice" by asking questions in meetings, attending meetups, and building a digital footprint on platforms like LinkedIn [37:50].

5. Encouraging Women in STEM
Finding Your Niche: Su shares that she actually came from a marketing background, proving that you don't need to be a traditional "scientist" to lead in tech; every field now has a STEM element [24:41].

Courage over Perfection: She notes that women often don't apply for roles unless they tick every box. She advises "taking courage" to apply anyway and positioning yourself through your unique interests [35:11].

Conclusion:
Su’s final message is one of authenticity. While the tech landscape is a "race," she advises professionals not to compare themselves to others but to focus on winning the right opportunities for themselves by remaining "human" in an increasingly automated world [40:59].

Title: resilience and courage - riting your own life story
Session Summary:
The video titled "Resilience and Courage - Writing your own life story" features Madhura Sinha, the Founder and CEO of Aspire For Her, along with her colleagues Preeti Bhaskar and Hepsiba. The session, hosted by "Girls Leading Tech" for International Women's Day, focuses on empowering women to achieve economic independence and leadership, particularly in the technology sector.

1. The Vision of Aspire For Her
Origin Story: Madhura started the organization after a 25-year banking career [02:19]. She was motivated by personal stories of women—one unable to pay for her father's cancer treatment [03:41] and another pressured to quit her job for marriage [04:26]—highlighting the dire need for financial independence.

The Goal: The organization initially aimed to bring 1 million women into the workforce by 2025, a goal they achieved early in October 2025 [06:23]. Their new "audacious goal" is to add 10 million women by 2030 [08:01].

Mission: It is not about "women vs. men" but about enhancing the country's economy through gender parity [07:20].

2. The "E³ = MC³" Framework
Madhura explains their unique model for building communities and supporting women [09:20]:

E³ (The Goals): Employment, Entrepreneurship, and Education.

M: Mentors and Role Models [09:50].

C¹: Courses, Cohorts, and Learning (focusing on upskilling for the AI-driven future) [10:12].

C²: Career Previews and Resources (celebrating success stories of diverse women) [10:45].

C³: Community (creating a safe space where women cheer for each other) [11:49].

3. Specialized Communities and Initiatives
Diverse Groups: They have 28 communities including "Women in Tech," "Women Returning to Work," and "Enterpre-naari" (partnered with Navya Naveli Nanda) [13:48].

"Not Alone" Community: Launched during the second wave of COVID-19 to support women who lost primary breadwinners [14:47]. Madhura shares the story of Preeti Kate from a small village who became an Abacus teacher and entrepreneur after her husband's passing [16:34].

New Launch: "Women Who Master": Preeti Bhaskar introduces their 29th community in partnership with Logitech [21:38]. This includes an all-women hackathon across four zones in India, with the grand finale in Chennai [22:41].

4. Personal Stories of Resilience
Hepsiba’s Journey: Hepsiba shares her struggle with a 7-year career gap and health issues after COVID-19 [26:54]. Despite "brain fog" and initial rejections due to her gap, she reached out to Madhura on LinkedIn and eventually joined the team as a Project Coordinator [27:46].

Defining Courage: Madhura defines courage not as the absence of fear, but as inner conviction [30:15]. She emphasizes taking the first step even when the future is uncertain [31:12].

Limiting Scripts: The speakers discuss how societal expectations (e.g., "sales isn't a girl's job") act as limiting scripts [36:29]. They encourage women to build inner strength rather than trying to fix the entire ecosystem first [33:33].

5. Closing Message
Madhura views her life in 25-year chapters: first as a student, then in corporate banking, and now, starting at age 50, writing her most important chapter with Aspire For Her [38:14]. She invites the audience to join as mentors for underprivileged girls and participate in the upcoming tech initiatives [40:47].

Title: courage above all
Session Summary:
The video titled "Courage Above All ft Jacintha Jayachandran," hosted by the "Girls Leading Tech" channel, features a powerful keynote by Jacintha Jayachandran, the Founder-CEO of HopeWorks Foundation. The talk is a critique of societal expectations placed on women and a call to action for authentic, courageous living.

Core Message: Moving Beyond Societal Labels
Jacintha opens by expressing her fatigue with traditional International Women's Day tropes that celebrate women for their "silent suffering" or ability to "manage everything with a smile" [02:17]. She argues that these are societal constructs she never signed up for [04:18].

Key Lessons on Courage
Jacintha outlines several "lessons of courage" derived from her professional and personal journey:

Resign from Unwanted Expectations: She encourages women to "resign" from roles and ideas thrust upon them by others. If you didn't consciously sign up for a responsibility, you have the right to let it go [06:11].

Embrace Authentic Emotions: She advocates for the freedom to express genuine anger, sadness, or joy. There is no virtue in "tolerating" everything; if someone ticks you off, it is courageous to say so [07:36].

Build a Resource System: Since the world is not "waiting to save you," you must create your own support systems—whether through friends, family, or solo time to recharge [10:03].

Respect Your Mind and Body: Jacintha emphasizes that your mind and body are your only lifelong companions. Listening to them—whether they signal fatigue or a need for a different "power dress"—is an act of self-respect [13:44].

Be Your Own Fan: She suggests women should be the "star on the screen and the fan in the gallery" [16:43]. This involves defending yourself against self-criticism by "dredging up data" of your past successes, much like a sports fan defends a superstar [18:50].

Q&A and Career Insights
In the latter half of the session, Jacintha answers questions regarding career and leadership:

Finding Your Dream: In response to a viewer feeling overwhelmed by "noise," she explains that dreams evolve. The key is to find what feels "natural" and puts you in a "flow" state [27:39].

Leading with Heart and Intuition: She dismisses the idea of "data vs. intuition," suggesting that the best leaders operate in the "gray area" where they validate patterns (intuition) with facts (data) [30:24].

The Act of Resilience: For those facing adversity, she defines courage as simply "putting one foot in front of the other" every morning rather than performing grand heroic acts [33:06].

The Power of Defiance: To early-career professionals, she advises being "defiant" [36:27]. While society is designed to make people "fit in," standing out requires the courage to defy norms that don't serve you.

The video concludes with a call to lead with courage every day of the year, rather than just accepting recognition on a single designated day [23:46].

Title: recruiters insights: what top tech firms want
Session Summary:
This video features Chaitra Rao, Founder and Managing Partner of People Impact, sharing her 24+ years of experience in executive search and talent acquisition. She provides a comprehensive guide for students and professionals looking to build meaningful careers in the evolving tech industry.

The Foundation: Knowing Your "Why"
Rao emphasizes that "knowing what you want is half the battle won" [02:44].

Self-Discovery: Before diving into a career, students should identify what they genuinely enjoy and what comes naturally to them. She notes that many students pursue engineering due to parental pressure rather than personal interest [05:41].

Starting Small: You don't need a 100% fixed plan on day one. Start with small decisions—experiments, asking the right questions, and exploring interests to set a general direction [05:12].

What Top Tech Firms Really Want
The speaker shifts the focus from traditional academic credentials to practical skill sets.

Skills Over Degrees: While a degree is a "hygiene factor," tech hiring is now extremely skill-led and outcome-driven [08:17].

Closing the Industry Gap: There is often a disconnect between theoretical college syllabi and job requirements. Rao suggests bridge courses, certifications (from authentic entities), and hands-on projects [11:25].

The Coding Test: 9 out of 10 tech roles involve a coding test. Rao advises that your first time seeing a coding problem shouldn't be at the actual interview; practice via hackathons and online platforms is essential [10:41].

Building a Professional Brand
In a competitive market like India, "being visible" is crucial [13:01].

LinkedIn Optimization: Recruiters invariably check LinkedIn. A strong profile should include a professional photo, a clear objective, and a summary of your journey [14:13].

Social Media Scrutiny: Candidates must be mindful of their online presence. Offensive or insensitive posts can lead to immediate disqualification during leadership hiring [16:10].

Networking: Use platforms to connect with "gurus" and communities in your specific niche (e.g., Cloud Architecture or DevOps) to stay ahead of industry trends [17:46].

Soft Skills and "The Human Element"
Technical proficiency is only part of the equation. Firms look for:

Comfort with Ambiguity: Unlike college, where everything is structured, jobs are often undefined. You must be comfortable "figuring things out" on your own [26:43].

The Relationship with Failure: Rao views a lack of failure as a "red flag," especially for senior roles. It suggests a lack of risk-taking or an inability to admit mistakes [28:07].

Befriending AI: Instead of fearing that AI will take jobs, candidates should "champion" it. Use AI to move from "1x to 3x" productivity [23:43].

Interpersonal Skills: As you grow, it's not about "I," but "we." Articulation and the ability to influence outcomes are non-negotiable for leadership [30:13].

Advice for Women in Tech
Rao highlights that while the number of women in tech has grown from 2-3% to higher percentages over the decades, there is still a long way to go [36:34]. She encourages women to leverage specific outreach programs, such as Grace Hopper, and corporate-sponsored education initiatives [37:07].

Q&A Insights
On Final Year Projects: While having a copyright or IP for a project shows conviction and "real work," it won't be the only factor in getting recruited; it's an "extra" plus [39:07].

On "Bar Raisers": To stand out at firms like Google or Microsoft, you must think a few steps ahead of your peer group and have a clear 5-to-10-year career goal [41:08].

On Growth: Don't rush into management just for the title. Some people are better off as individual contributors (engineers) for 30 years, which can be just as lucrative [30:41].

Title: the career compass - prepare and practice
Session Summary:
This video features Sobhitha Neelanath, a Senior Engineering Leader at Salesforce, sharing a comprehensive career guide titled "Career Compass: Prepare and Practice." The session is structured around three core pillars: Building a Foundation, Creating a Personal Brand, and Ensuring Long-term Growth.

1. Showing Up: Building a Strong Foundation
A career path is only as stable as its foundation. Sobhitha emphasizes that early preparation is key to a successful start [04:02].

Core Competence: Develop in-depth knowledge in your chosen field (e.g., Data Structures and Algorithms for software engineering, or specific skills for HR and Finance) [04:36].

Dynamic Resumes: Beyond templates, focus on "demonstrable skills." Your projects and internships should highlight practical experience rather than just theoretical concepts [06:40].

Interview Articulation: Technical knowledge is insufficient without clear communication. Practicing mock interviews and refining your articulation is vital [08:02].

Identifying "Super Powers": Use self-reflection and feedback from peers or mentors to identify your unique strengths [10:04].

2. Standing Out: Building Your Career Brand
In a competitive market, you must differentiate yourself through visibility and initiative [11:53].

Leverage Digital Presence: Maintain a professional LinkedIn profile. Post about college events, leadership roles, and initiatives to show you are "driven by initiative" [13:51].

Showcase Impact: Build a portfolio (e.g., a GitHub profile) and contribute to open-source or publish research in journals like IEEE to provide tangible proof of your work [14:53].

The "Elevator Pitch": Sobhitha explains the concept of a 30-45 second summary that highlights your Unique Selling Proposition (USP), skills, and the impact you’ve made [18:00].

Post-Interview Etiquette: A simple but rare way to stand out is sending a personalized "thank you" note to your interviewer [13:17].

3. Staying Ahead: Long-term Growth
Sobhitha describes a career as a marathon, not a sprint, requiring consistency and stamina [23:08].

Continuous Learning: Stay updated with industry trends like AI. You don't need to be an expert in everything, but you must know how to leverage new tech effectively in your role [24:07].

The Power of Mentorship: Having multiple mentors (alumni, seniors, or industry professionals) helps navigate the "unwritten rules" of corporate culture [26:22].

Relationship Management: Success in a corporate environment depends heavily on team dynamics and having a strong rapport with your manager [27:58].

Proactive Experimentation: Don't shy away from challenges. Failing early in your career is an opportunity to bounce back and learn faster [29:44].

4. Q&A and Key Takeaways
AI in Quality Engineering: Sobhitha notes that AI is shifting testing from a simple "pass/fail" metric to focusing on accuracy and continuous model training [33:53].

Grace Hopper Celebration (GHCI): She discusses the importance of the AnitaB.org community and how students can apply for scholarships to attend technical conferences like GHCI for free [35:27].

Book Recommendation: She highly recommends Atomic Habits by James Clear, emphasizing the importance of building systems and habits rather than just chasing goals [38:30].

Action Plan Summary:

Update LinkedIn regularly with your latest achievements [30:58].

Schedule a discussion with a professional in your target field [31:20].

Commit to learning one new skill every month to stay relevant [31:51].

Title: how to embrace yourself and discorver your purpose
Session Summary:
This detailed summary of the video "How to Embrace Yourself and Discover Your Purpose" featuring Bhavana Issar, Founder and CEO of Caregiver Saathi, explores her journey and insights on building a life driven by purpose.

1. Redefining Life's Purpose
Bhavana challenges the notion that there is only one grand purpose in life. Instead, she suggests:

Purpose is Contextual: At different life stages, your purpose changes. For a student, it may be learning; for a professional, it may be career building and family support [02:49].

Dynamic Meaning: Purpose is the meaning we give to our lives at any specific point in time. It is not a fixed destination but a continuous addition of dimensions to who we are [03:40].

2. The Journey of Self-Discovery
To find one's purpose, Bhavana emphasizes answering the fundamental question: "Who am I?"

The "Who I Am" vs. "Who I Wish to Be" Gap: Identify the traits you currently possess and those you aspire to have. The actions you take to bridge this gap define your goals and provide a sense of purpose [07:06].

The Power of Detours: Life often takes unexpected turns. Bhavana shared her own transition from studying Computer Science in the 90s to an HR career, and eventually founding a social enterprise [11:46]. She encourages embracing these "detours" as learning experiences that lead back to your core [13:20].

3. Three Qualities for Success
Citing Rshami Bansal’s book Touch the Sky (which features Bhavana’s story), she highlights three traits often found in women who write their own destinies [17:32]:

Ziddi (Stubborn): Being persistent about your goals.

Besharm (Shameless): Not being held back by societal judgment.

Bindas (Carefree): Living life on your own terms.

4. Practical Frameworks for Self-Growth
Bhavana introduces several methods for self-reflection and embracing oneself:

The Johari Window: A tool to understand how you see yourself versus how others perceive you. Seeking feedback helps crystallize your true identity [25:12].

The "Soft Eyes" Approach: Embracing yourself means accepting your failures and flaws without judgment, looking at yourself with "soft" or "kind" eyes [31:26].

The Power of the Pause: In a world of constant stimulation, learning to slow down and meditate allows you to reflect on your actions and emotions [22:20].

5. Life Advice and "The Compound Effect"
Drawing from her two decades of HR experience, Bhavana offers crucial advice for young people:

Jobs Don't Define You: Never let a job interview or performance review define your worth. Your identity is much larger than your professional role [45:20].

Nurture Diverse Dimensions: Just like financial planning, your health, relationships, and skills benefit from compounding. What you invest in your 20s—socially, physically, and mentally—will yield massive returns in your 40s and 50s [47:24].

Embrace Your "Weirdness": Everyone has a "personal weirdness index." Instead of trying to fit into a box, you should celebrate your unique traits [49:17].

Book Recommendation
Bhavana recommends "Jonathan Livingston Seagull" by Richard Bach, a story about believing in oneself and striving for excellence [50:45].

Title: introduction to ai careers
Session Summary:
The video, titled "Introduction to AI Careers with Yashaswini Vismaya," is an in-depth session focused on navigating the evolving landscape of Artificial Intelligence (AI) and Machine Learning (ML). Yashaswini Vismaya, an AI engineer with 15 years of experience, provides a comprehensive roadmap for students and professionals looking to enter the field.

1. The AI Landscape & Evolution
Yashaswini begins by clarifying that AI is an umbrella term encompassing machines that replicate human intelligence, dating back to the 1950s [01:59]. She breaks down the field into several key paradigms:

Machine Learning (ML): Categorized into Supervised (labeled data), Unsupervised (pattern finding), and Reinforcement Learning (reward/penalty systems) [02:20].

Deep Learning: Inspired by human neurons, specifically mentioning CNNs for image processing and RNNs for Natural Language Processing (NLP) [03:41].

Generative AI: A major turning point in the last 2–3 years where AI creates new content (images, audio, video) using Large Language Models (LLMs) [04:52].

Agentic Systems: The current frontier (2025–2026), focusing on multi-agent systems and orchestration [05:39].

2. AI Career Path Framework
The speaker uses a "flowchart" approach to help viewers identify which role suits them best based on their interests [06:46]:

AI Builders: Focus on development and deployment.

AI Developer: Focuses on programming and ML frameworks [07:40].

ML Engineer: Focuses on system architecture and cloud platforms [07:52].

Research Innovators:

Research Scientist: Requires advanced mathematics and algorithm development [08:08].

Domain Specialists: Focus on industry-specific AI solutions and analysis [08:40].

AI Ethicists & Strategists: Focus on policy, governance, and business transformation [09:17].

3. Progression & Levels of Expertise
She details how roles evolve from level 1 (beginner) to level 5 (leader) [11:16]:

AI Product Manager: Bridges technical capability with business strategy and roadmaps [15:06].

Ethical AI Advocate: Emphasizes that ethics must "come from within," focusing on safety frameworks and policy [16:07].

Data Scientist (AI Focused): Evolves from statistical analysis to driving data-centric cultures in organizations [15:33].

4. Advice for Aspiring AI Professionals
During the Q&A, Yashaswini shares actionable advice for students to differentiate themselves [18:20]:

Curiosity & Proactiveness: These are the primary traits recruiters look for [18:57].

Hands-on Portfolio: * Build a GitHub repository for all projects—it serves as your digital brand [24:06].

Participate in Hackathons and Internships; one real-world project is worth four years of engineering theory [27:06].

Use Kaggle for datasets and competitions [20:22].

Networking Strategy: * Use LinkedIn to follow industry trends and connect with experts [21:49].

"Your network is your net worth" [29:14].

Collaborate to Leverage: Once you have a network, collaborate on research papers or hackathons rather than just collecting connections [30:07].

Altruistic Mindset: Adopt a "give to gain" philosophy. Sharing opportunities and helping others eventually adds value back to your own career [30:48].

Summary Conclusion
The core message is to view the AI revolution not as a threat, but as an opportunity for creativity and productivity [21:02]. To stay relevant for the next decade, students must move beyond textbooks, engage with industry professionals through podcasts and meetups, and maintain a constant thirst for learning the "latest and greatest" models [25:36].

Title: the power of public speaking
Session Summary:
The video, titled "The power of Public Speaking with Bobbie Carlton," is a comprehensive session from the Empower Her 2.0 event. Bobbie Carlton, founder of Innovation Women and a TEDx speaker, discusses the critical importance of public speaking for women's career advancement and provides actionable advice on how to overcome barriers to the stage.

The Gender Gap in Public Speaking
Bobbie highlights a stark reality: two-thirds of all conference speakers are male [02:43]. This leads to the "manel" (all-male panel) phenomenon, which limits women's visibility and reinforces the idea that only men are industry leaders. She emphasizes that you cannot be seen as a leader if you are always sitting in the audience [04:47].

The 5 C's of Public Speaking
Carlton breaks down the benefits of public speaking into five key areas:

Competence: Being on stage allows you to demonstrate your knowledge and expertise [07:29].

Confidence: Confidence is built through speaking, not before. Even as an introvert, Bobbie notes that the more you speak, the more confident you become in all areas of life [07:52].

Connections: Speakers are the "content" of an event, placing them at the center of marketing efforts and providing networking opportunities even before they step on stage [08:50].

Credibility: When you are on a physical stage, people literally and figuratively "look up to you," granting you immediate expert status [10:40].

Cash: While 50% of event managers have no speaker budget [12:06], public speaking correlates with higher salaries and business growth through increased visibility [12:37].

How to Get Started
There are millions of speaking opportunities annually, ranging from local meetups to international conferences [13:25]. Bobbie suggests several ways to "stick a toe in the water":

Ask a Question: Simply standing up in the audience to ask an intelligent question makes you a public speaker to that same audience [17:00].

Vary the Format: You don't have to start with a solo keynote. Consider being a panelist, a co-presenter, a podcast guest, or a moderator [15:31].

Find Your "Signature Talk": Create a presentation that sits at the intersection of your expertise, your passion, and the audience's interest (specifically their "pain points") [23:21].

Overcoming Barriers and Nerves
Women often say "no" to speaking due to practical reasons: working for smaller companies, lack of travel budget, or childcare responsibilities [27:39]. Bobbie urges women to say "yes" to normalize seeing women on stage [34:25].

Tips for Managing Fear:

Reframe Anxiety: Tell yourself, "I'm not nervous, I'm excited." The physical sensation is nearly identical [32:33].

The Tennis Ball Trick: For those who freeze, Bobbie suggests a coaching technique: focus on the feeling of a (metaphorical) tennis ball under the arch of your foot or grip the stage with your toes to ground yourself [38:15].

Take a Beat: If you freeze on stage, take a slow drink of water. It feels like an eternity to you, but it’s a natural pause to the audience [41:15].

Conclusion
Bobbie concludes by encouraging the audience to seek out practice through Toastmasters or improv classes [42:15]. Her ultimate goal is to close the gender gap on professional stages, ensuring women are recognized as the experts and leaders they are.

Title: Become a leader with Aspire Leadership Program
Session Summary:
The video features a detailed session with Sashi Gundala, Director of Aspire India, where she shares her career journey and provides deep insights into the Aspire Leaders Program, an initiative affiliated with Harvard University.

1. Sashi Gundala’s Career Journey [01:45]
Sashi describes her transition from a traditional engineering background in India to becoming a leader in the U.S. and India:

Early Career: Initially pressured by her parents to become a doctor or engineer, she chose engineering and moved to the U.S. [01:53].

Entrepreneurship: She started her own computer training institute in the U.S., teaching skills like C++, Oracle, and Unix [02:36].

Pivot to Education: Following her passion, she earned a Master’s in Education and a leadership degree, working as a teacher, principal, and director in the U.S. school system for nearly 30 years [03:25].

Return to India: Three years ago, she returned to India to work in social impact, eventually becoming the first Director for Aspire India in January 2025 [04:04].

2. Overcoming Challenges as a Woman Leader [07:12]
Sashi discusses the hurdles of being a minority woman in leadership:

Proving Worth: She emphasizes that women often have to work harder to be taken seriously for leadership roles [07:21].

Cultural Adaptation: To succeed as an Indian woman in the U.S. public school system, she learned to "play the role"—adapting her dress and communication style to fit the professional environment while maintaining her personal cultural roots [08:17].

Confidence as a Key: She defines leadership appearance through confidence, advising students to "own the room" and use a firm handshake [12:38].

3. The Aspire Leaders Program [14:15]
The core of the video is an explanation of the Aspire program, which aims to empower first-generation college students:

Curriculum: Delivered by faculty from Harvard and Stanford, it covers employability skills, life skills, and problem-solving [14:29].

Eligibility: Open to students aged 18–29 from limited-income families. The program is completely free [19:31].

Structure: Includes a 9-week course and a capstone project that students can feature on their resumes to demonstrate collaboration and technical skills [16:22].

4. Lifelong Alumni Benefits [17:13]
Once students complete the program, they become "alumni for life" and gain access to:

Employment & Internships: Partnerships with companies like Mahindra, Porter, and Kabil (a woman-led organization) [17:42].

Grants & Scholarships: Aspire provides financial aid ranging from ₹25,000 to ₹10 Lakhs for competitive exams, certifications (e.g., AWS, Google, PMP), or social impact projects [18:21].

Seed Money: Financial backing for students looking to start their own businesses or community projects [18:44].

5. Professional Advice for Students [28:31]
Sashi offers practical tips for navigating the professional world:

Beyond the Degree: Corporates look for "employability skills"—how you talk, present ideas, and communicate via email [29:20].

LinkedIn Etiquette: She warns against sending simple "Hi" messages. Instead, students should introduce themselves properly, state their purpose, and ask for specific help [30:36].

Multitasking vs. Focus: While multitasking works for some, she advises others to focus on doing one thing properly to ensure success [25:40].

The session concludes with an interactive Q&A where students ask about specific grants for healthcare and STEM projects, and Sashi encourages them to apply for the upcoming cohort [33:53].
